summary: Re-research OSS/commercial TUI+GUI remoting to derive lowest-latency, native-feel design for our 2 paths

synthesis.headlineRecommendation: Build PATH 1 first and ship it before touching PATH 2: a libghostty external-IO surface (use_custom_io fork: wiedymi/daiimus) fed raw PTY bytes over plain TCP with TCP_NODELAY, dual data/control channels, and an Eternal-Terminal-style 64 MB sequence-numbered replay buffer for lossless reconnect. At a 5-20 ms NetBird RTT this already feels local; do NOT build a Mosh shadow-framebuffer predictor for v1 (the opaque ghostty_surface_t forces a full duplicate VT parser whose desync risk outweighs a 5-20 ms saving that only applies at the bare shell prompt). For PATH 2, the single highest-impact decision is client-side cursor rendering (strip cursor from capture, send position+bitmap on a tiny side channel, composite on the client at display refresh) so pointer latency equals RTT regardless of the encode/decode pipeline; pair it with a native VTCompressionSession HEVC low-latency recipe, SCFrameStatus.idle skip, and lossy-first/lossless-upgrade for text legibility. CGEvent injection and the video-region-to-host coordinate mapping remain the one unsolved risk and must be spiked before PATH 2 is committed.
synthesis.terminalDesign: SEE structured terminalDesign field above (this duplicate key is required by schema; primary content is in the terminalDesign property).
synthesis.guiDesign:
SCOPE: PATH 2 is a strictly separate pipeline from PATH 1 — do not try to unify the libghostty surface with video. Build it as Phase 4, after PATH 1 ships.

CAPTURE: ScreenCaptureKit with SCContentFilter(window:) for per-window capture (on macOS 14+ this captures the window backing store, correct regardless of occlusion — verify on target OS). Set SCStreamConfiguration.pixelFormat = kCVPixelFormatType_420YpCbCr8BiPlanarVideoRange (NV12) for zero-copy hand-off to a native VTCompressionSession (avoid the BGRA->NV12 conversion that FFmpeg-wrapped paths like Lumen incur). queueDepth = 5 (the proven Sunshine/Apple-sample value; the "3 is minimum" claim is unverified). minimumFrameInterval = CMTime(1,30). Honor the SCKit release constraint: each surface must be released within minimumFrameInterval x (queueDepth-1) (~133 ms at 30fps/depth5) — release the CMSampleBuffer surface immediately after handing the CVPixelBuffer to the encoder. Set SCStreamConfiguration.showsCursor = false so the cursor is stripped from captured frames (this is the per-window-correct way; do NOT use system-wide CGDisplayHideCursor).

CODEC: native VTCompressionSession (NOT an FFmpeg wrapper), HEVC 8-bit 4:2:0 (kVTProfileLevel_HEVC_Main_AutoLevel). In the encoderSpecification dict at creation time (these are Specification keys, not Property keys — the common trap): kVTVideoEncoderSpecification_EnableLowLatencyRateControl = true (confirmed valid for HEVC on Apple Silicon, our host), kVTVideoEncoderSpecification_RequireHardwareAcceleratedVideoEncoder = true (hard-fail rather than silent software fallback). Via VTSessionSetProperty: RealTime=true, ExpectedFrameRate=30, PrioritizeEncodingSpeedOverQuality=true (Apple's exact 4-flag low-latency recipe), AllowFrameReordering=false (no B-frames), MaxKeyFrameInterval=INT_MAX (IDR on demand only). EnableLowLatencyRateControl requires an explicit target bitrate (returns EINVAL at 0). Do NOT set max_ref_frames=1 — harmless on HEVC but a trap if H.264 is ever used (causes all-IDR, ~3x bandwidth). Do not query UsingHardwareAcceleratedVideoEncoder while low-latency mode is on (returns -12900).

LOSSY-FIRST / LOSSLESS-UPGRADE (the highest-value GUI quality mechanism for a coding tool): on each SCFrameStatus.complete, encode at quality ~0.65 and send immediately so first-frame appears at ~RTT; accumulate the dirty-rect union and start a GCD DispatchSourceTimer (~200-600 ms, computed as max(batch_delay x5, 200 ms) — NOT the 1000 ms figure, which is Xpra's iconic-window batch lock, not its refresh timer); reschedule on each new frame; when it fires after true idle, re-encode the dirty union with kVTCompressionPropertyKey_Lossless=true (Apple-Silicon-only; do not rely on quality=1.0 which still subsamples chroma) as an INTRA/IDR slice so UDP loss of the upgrade cannot corrupt decode state. Window chrome (height < 40 px, title/scroll bars) always encodes lossless on the first pass.

DAMAGE TRACKING: in didOutputSampleBuffer read SCStreamFrameInfo.status; if == SCFrameStatus.idle, return immediately (no IOSurface, no encode, no send) — this is the zero-cost damage check and keeps the encoder slot vacant so the next real frame gets hardware immediately. Send a heartbeat IDR ~every 1 s on otherwise-idle windows.

TRANSPORT + LOSS: plain UDP over NetBird (no DTLS/QUIC — WireGuard already does ChaCha20-Poly1305; inner crypto is pure overhead). 4-byte sequence number per packet for ordering/loss detection. Reed-Solomon FEC ~20% parity per frame (Sunshine default). Prefer LTR-frame loss recovery (kVTCompressionPropertyKey_EnableLTR + ForceLTRRefresh on loss) over forcing IDRs — needs a tiny client->host ACK channel, cheap at our RTT, with a 2-RTT timeout fallback to IDR. Cap encode queue at 2-3 frames in flight; drop oldest, never backpressure the SCKit callback thread.

CLIENT-SIDE CURSOR (single highest-impact native-feel technique): cursor is excluded from video (showsCursor=false); host samples NSEvent mouse position at ~120 Hz and sends position(host-space CGPoint)+shape(NSCursor image+hotspot) on a SEPARATE <64-byte UDP socket (never multiplexed onto the video socket, or video backpressure delays it). Client composites the cursor as a Metal quad / CALayer over the decoded frame at display refresh. Cursor latency = RTT only, decoupled from encode/decode entirely (Parsec US11750674; Selkies pattern, not the moonlight-qt SDL_ShowCursor toggle).

FRAME PACING: drive client display from CADisplayLink (VSync), not from decode completion; hold the last decoded frame when the queue is empty (Moonlight pacer: TIMER_SLACK ~3 ms, show-last-frame on empty, time-critical thread). Decode session RealTime=true; CVMetalTextureCache for zero-copy CVPixelBuffer->Metal; CAMetalLayer.maximumDrawableCount=2 to hold display latency to ~one vsync. Verify decode is not silently 2-frame-buffered on real Apple-Silicon hardware.

INPUT: CGEventPost(kCGHIDEventTap, ...) for clicks/keys (requires the Accessibility toggle granted pre-launch; no sandbox, no Mac App Store distribution) and CGWarpMouseCursorPosition for absolute moves; tag every injected event with eventSourceUserData so the host's own taps can filter self-injected events. Mouse-move coalescing (drain pending moves, accumulate deltas, one send — Moonlight SDL_PeepEvents pattern). The load-bearing correctness risk is mapping client video-region coordinates back to host-window/screen coordinates exactly — a mismatch makes the cursor appear in one place while clicks register in another.

nativeFeelTechniques.technique: Client-side cursor rendering: strip cursor from capture (showsCursor=false), transmit position+bitmap on a dedicated <64-byte UDP side channel, composite locally at display refresh
nativeFeelTechniques.impact: high — decouples pointer latency from the entire encode/decode/transport pipeline; cursor tracks at RTT (5-20ms) instead of 30-50ms. Confirmed independently by 3 families (Parsec US11750674, Moonlight, Selkies). This is the single difference between 'feels remote' and 'feels local' for GUI use.
nativeFeelTechniques.effort: medium — separate UDP socket, host NSEvent sampling at 120Hz, client Metal cursor quad; coordinate-space mapping is the fiddly part

nativeFeelTechniques.technique: TCP_NODELAY on every PATH 1 socket immediately after connect
nativeFeelTechniques.impact: high — without it Nagle batches single-keystroke writes and can add up to 200ms; this single line is the difference between local-feel and laggy typing. Was the one omission across the surveyed terminal stacks.
nativeFeelTechniques.effort: trivial — one setsockopt call per socket

nativeFeelTechniques.technique: libghostty external-IO backend (use_custom_io + feed_data/write_callback) over raw TCP PTY stream
nativeFeelTechniques.impact: high — gives full-fidelity native terminal rendering with event-driven redraw and zero protocol round-trips; raw-byte echo at 5-20ms RTT is already indistinguishable from local. Proven by VVTerm and Geistty.
nativeFeelTechniques.effort: medium-high — requires maintaining a patched ghostty fork; functions absent from upstream

nativeFeelTechniques.technique: SCFrameStatus.idle skip — suppress encode+send entirely on unchanged frames; heartbeat IDR ~1s
nativeFeelTechniques.impact: high — >90% of frames in a coding session are static; skipping them keeps the hardware encoder slot vacant so the next real keystroke-driven frame encodes immediately, and slashes bandwidth. Zero-cost native damage signal.

nativeFeelTechniques.technique: Lossy-first then lossless-upgrade (quality 0.65 immediately, kVTCompressionPropertyKey_Lossless intra-slice after idle) on dirty-rect union
nativeFeelTechniques.impact: high — solves the fast-vs-legible dilemma for a coding tool: response at RTT speed, then pixel-perfect text once the user pauses to read. Upgrade sent as IDR slice so UDP loss can't corrupt decode.
nativeFeelTechniques.effort: medium — dirty-rect accumulation, reschedulable GCD timer, two encode passes

nativeFeelTechniques.technique: VideoToolbox 4-flag low-latency recipe (EnableLowLatencyRateControl + RealTime + ExpectedFrameRate + PrioritizeEncodingSpeedOverQuality) on a native VTCompressionSession with NV12 zero-copy capture
nativeFeelTechniques.impact: high — one-in-one-out encode, no reorder buffer, ~5-18ms HEVC encode on Apple Silicon, eliminates the BGRA conversion step. Keeps total motion-to-photon inside one 30fps frame slot.
nativeFeelTechniques.effort: medium — careful CFDictionary bridging from Swift; Specification-vs-Property key placement is a trap

nativeFeelTechniques.technique: ET BackedWriter sequence-numbered 64MB replay buffer for lossless reconnect
nativeFeelTechniques.impact: high — survives iOS backgrounding / network blips with byte-exact resume and no server-side tmux dependency; essential for a mobile client that sleeps constantly.
nativeFeelTechniques.effort: medium — host ring buffer + seq numbering + reconnect handshake

nativeFeelTechniques.technique: CADisplayLink/VSync-driven frame release with show-last-frame on empty queue (Moonlight pacer)
nativeFeelTechniques.impact: medium-high — eliminates judder and tearing, holds display latency to ~one vsync; late frames are skipped rather than queued, preventing latency accumulation.
nativeFeelTechniques.effort: low-medium — CADisplayLink callback + last-frame hold + time-critical thread

nativeFeelTechniques.technique: iOS terminal table stakes: DispatchSourceTimer key-repeat (350/50ms), separate UITextView IME proxy, UITextInput floating-cursor (5pt threshold), keyboard-gated accessory bar
nativeFeelTechniques.impact: medium-high — without these, held-key navigation breaks, CJK input breaks, and iPhone cursor movement is impossible; these are the gap between 'works' and 'feels like a native iOS app'.
nativeFeelTechniques.effort: medium — several well-documented UIKit patterns from Blink/VVTerm/SwiftTerm

nativeFeelTechniques.technique: Dual data/control TCP channels (PTY bytes vs TIOCSWINSZ resize + intent)
nativeFeelTechniques.impact: medium — prevents a Claude Code output burst from delaying a window-resize ack; keeps the terminal responsive during heavy output.
nativeFeelTechniques.effort: low — second framed channel or multiplexed sub-stream

nativeFeelTechniques.technique: Glitch-window speculative caret (cursor-column-only, dim caret advance when no echo within ~150-250ms)
nativeFeelTechniques.impact: medium — gives 'input received' feedback during Claude Code stalls without the cost/desync risk of a full Mosh shadow framebuffer; the cheap 80% of the prediction benefit.
nativeFeelTechniques.effort: low — track cursor column only, no full VT shadow parser

nativeFeelTechniques.technique: LTR-frame loss recovery + ~20% Reed-Solomon FEC instead of forced IDR on loss
nativeFeelTechniques.impact: medium — avoids the bandwidth/latency spike of keyframe recovery; near-irrelevant at NetBird's near-zero loss but costs nothing and hardens against WiFi clients.
nativeFeelTechniques.effort: medium — client->host ACK channel + FEC encode, 2-RTT IDR fallback

nativeFeelTechniques.technique: QOS_CLASS_USER_INTERACTIVE / critical thread priority on PTY relay and capture+encode threads
nativeFeelTechniques.impact: medium — keeps the latency-critical paths scheduled ahead of background work; mandatory to stay inside the 30fps frame budget on PATH 2.

gapAnalysis.item: PATH 1: raw VT byte stream over plain TCP, libghostty renders
gapAnalysis.rationale: Correct and confirmed by VVTerm/Geistty; at 5-20ms RTT raw echo is already local-feel with no protocol round-trips.

gapAnalysis.item: PATH 1: no app-layer encryption (WireGuard handles it)
gapAnalysis.rationale: Correct; inner crypto over WireGuard is pure overhead with zero security benefit.

gapAnalysis.item: PATH 1: input = raw bytes to PTY stdin
gapAnalysis.rationale: Eliminates the input-injection problem entirely on the terminal path — a major architectural win to preserve.

gapAnalysis.item: PATH 1: adopt Mosh-style client-side speculative local echo
gapAnalysis.rationale: Do NOT build the full shadow-framebuffer predictor for v1 — opaque ghostty forces a duplicate VT parser whose desync risk outweighs a 5-20ms saving that only applies at the bare shell prompt; ship a cheap glitch-window caret instead, keep full prediction as a measured Phase-2 option.

gapAnalysis.item: PATH 1: libghostty as opaque full-surface renderer
gapAnalysis.rationale: Upstream libghostty cannot do external IO — feed_data/write_callback exist only in forks (wiedymi/daiimus); commit to maintaining a patched fork as a tracked dependency.

gapAnalysis.item: PATH 1 transport detail: TCP socket options
gapAnalysis.rationale: Set TCP_NODELAY immediately after connect — Nagle alone can add up to 200ms to single-keystroke writes; this was the one omission in the surveyed stacks.
Add ET-style 64MB sequence-numbered replay buffer for lossless resume across iOS backgrounding without mandating server-side tmux.
Separate resize/intent control channel so output bursts don't delay resize acks (Zellij lesson).
Key-repeat timer, separate IME-proxy UITextView, floating-cursor, accessory bar are table stakes; missing any breaks held-key nav, CJK, or iPhone cursor movement.
SCContentFilter(window:) on macOS 14+ captures backing store correctly even under occlusion; the right primitive.
Correct codec; EnableLowLatencyRateControl is now confirmed valid for HEVC on Apple Silicon (our host), not H.264-only.
Correct; no DTLS/QUIC needed inside WireGuard. Add only a 4-byte seq number for ordering/loss.
Idle-skip via SCFrameStatus.idle is the zero-cost damage check; 30fps frame slot (33ms) accommodates the ~20-50ms pipeline with thread-priority discipline.
Adopt Apple's exact 4-flag low-latency recipe + RequireHardwareAcceleratedVideoEncoder + NV12 zero-copy capture; place Specification keys in the creation dict, not via SetProperty.
Client-side cursor rendering (strip + side-channel + local composite) is the single highest-impact native-feel technique — pointer latency = RTT, fully decoupled from video.

gapAnalysis.item: PATH 2: text legibility on static editor windows
gapAnalysis.rationale: Lossy-first then lossless-upgrade (kVTCompressionPropertyKey_Lossless intra-slice after idle) gives fast response AND pixel-perfect code; without it you must choose blurry-or-slow.
A separate metadata channel must carry window move/resize/title so the client NSWindow repositions immediately, before the next video frame (confirmed by every per-window remoting solution).
Drive client display from CADisplayLink with show-last-frame on empty queue; never drive from decode completion (Moonlight pacer).
Prefer LTR-frame refresh + ~20% Reed-Solomon FEC over forced IDR; cheap at NetBird loss rates and hardens WiFi clients, encode lossless upgrade as IDR slice so loss can't corrupt decode.

gapAnalysis.item: PATH 2: CGEvent injection as input path
gapAnalysis.rationale: Confirmed the only viable macOS path (CGEventPost kCGHIDEventTap + CGWarpMouseCursorPosition), but accept its hard constraint: Accessibility permission, no sandbox, no Mac App Store — and treat video-region->host coordinate mapping as the primary correctness risk to spike.

openSpikes: Measure real keystroke echo latency end-to-end on the actual NetBird mesh (host PTY relay -> TCP -> shell echo -> libghostty render) at both 5ms and 20ms RTT; this single number decides whether the Phase-2 Mosh shadow-framebuffer predictor is ever needed.

Verify libghostty fork threading: can ghostty_surface_refresh/draw be safely driven from a background TCP receive thread, or must everything funnel through @MainActor? Confirms the redraw coalescing design and the fork's viability.

CGEvent injection coordinate fidelity: build a minimal harness that maps client video-region coordinates back to host-window/screen coordinates and confirm clicks land exactly where the composited cursor appears, including across Retina scaling and window moves. This is PATH 2's biggest correctness risk.

Measure VTDecompressionSession decode latency on real Apple-Silicon client hardware to confirm it is single-frame and not silently 2-frame-buffered (RealTime=true does not guarantee this) — determines whether the 30fps motion-to-photon budget actually holds.

Confirm SCStreamConfiguration.showsCursor=false cleanly strips the cursor from per-window CMSampleBuffers on the target macOS version (the cursor may already be composited into the IOSurface), since client-side cursor rendering depends on it.

Benchmark concurrent VTCompressionSession count: how many per-window HEVC sessions run on hardware before falling back to software (typically 2-4 on Apple Silicon) — sets the limit on simultaneous remote GUI windows.
Validate the ET-style 64MB replay-buffer reconnect handshake across a real iOS background->foreground cycle, including beginBackgroundTask suspend timing, to confirm lossless byte-exact resume.

Confirm kVTCompressionPropertyKey_Lossless availability/behavior for the lossless-upgrade pass on the target Apple-Silicon host and measure its frame size for a typical 1080p editor window to size the UDP send queue.

families.mechanism: Mosh PredictionEngine (speculative local echo over a shadow VT framebuffer) — specifically the `new_user_byte` / `cull` / `apply` triad operating on a client-maintained Framebuffer, combined with epoch-based misprediction recovery and adaptive SRTT-gated display
families.recommendationPath1:
Implement the Mosh PredictionEngine pattern on PATH 1 as follows:

Transport: plain TCP over NetBird, raw PTY byte stream (no Mosh SSP, no UDP). On the host: openpty + posix_spawn; stream all PTY output bytes over a single TCP connection. Send TIOCSWINSZ resize signals and session control (disconnect intent) on a separate framed control channel (second TCP stream or a multiplexed sub-channel) — this is the Zellij dual-channel lesson, prevents a burst of Claude Code output from delaying a resize acknowledgment.

Reconnect resilience: adopt ET's BackedWriter pattern — the host maintains an in-memory circular buffer (64 MB ceiling) of outgoing PTY byte packets tagged with a monotonic sequence number. On client reconnect, the client sends its last received sequence number; the host replays the tail. This gives lossless session resumption without UDP complexity, appropriate for a WireGuard mesh at 5-20 ms RTT with near-zero packet loss.

Speculative echo (the primary latency win): the client feeds the incoming PTY byte stream simultaneously to two consumers: (1) libghostty_surface_write (or equivalent input function) for rendering, and (2) a lightweight secondary VT parser maintaining a shadow Framebuffer (a plain cell grid of uint32_t codepoints + attribute bytes — approximately 80×24×8 = ~15 KB). On each keydown event, before the byte is sent to the host, run `PredictionEngine::new_user_byte(the_byte, shadow_framebuffer)`. The engine produces an overlay; apply it to the shadow framebuffer and signal libghostty to re-render from the updated state. When the host echo arrives and updates the shadow framebuffer, `cull()` confirms or kills the prediction within one RTT.

SRTT trigger configuration: at NetBird RTT of 5-20 ms, SRTT will be below SRTT_TRIGGER_HIGH (30 ms) almost always in Adaptive mode, so prediction is OFF by default. For a dedicated remote coding tool, set `display_preference = Always` (always show predictions regardless of SRTT) or `Experimental` (prediction_epoch = confirmed_epoch, self-confirming immediately — appropriate if misprediction visual snap is acceptable). `Always` with underline-until-confirmed is the safer UX choice for a coding tool since the user is predominantly typing at a shell prompt or inside Claude Code's TUI (predictable echo context).

Shadow parser: reuse an existing VT parser (e.g., the vt100 crate in Rust, or a minimal C implementation derived from Mosh's Parser::UTF8Parser). The parser sees the same raw byte stream as libghostty. It maintains the cursor position, current line content, and a dirty-row bitmap. The PredictionEngine reads from this shadow state; libghostty renders from its own internal state. When a prediction is confirmed (shadow framebuffer converges with prediction), libghostty already has the real bytes — no re-render needed. When a prediction is wrong (shadow snaps to server truth), trigger a libghostty re-render from the corrected shadow state.

The libghostty C API (ghostty_surface_t is `void*`, confirmed opaque — no cell-read function exists) makes the shadow parser mandatory. There is no alternative until a future ghostty VT-state read API is shipped.
families.recommendationPath2:
PATH 2 (ScreenCaptureKit / HEVC / UDP) is a video streaming path and the terminal-protocol family is not applicable. The mechanisms surveyed — speculative echo, VT framebuffer diffs, session reconnect replay — operate at the character-cell level and have no bearing on per-window GPU video frame capture and encode.

For PATH 2, the highest-value latency mechanism from adjacent research (not this family) is frame pacing and encode-to-send pipeline depth: VideoToolbox HEVC constant-quality encode should run with kVTCompressionPropertyKey_RealTime = true, and the UDP send should happen on a dedicated high-priority dispatch queue immediately in the output callback — not queued to a secondary thread. Idle-skip (suppress encode when the SCK frame is pixel-identical to the prior) reduces unnecessary encode latency on static windows. CGEvent injection remains the single biggest risk and is not addressed by anything in this terminal-protocol survey.
corrections: INACCURACY — become_tentative and CR: The survey states become_tentative fires 'on every Execute byte other than CR.' The actual source code (terminaloverlay.cc:797-804) shows that CR (0x0d) DOES call become_tentative() — it calls both become_tentative() AND newline_carriage_return(). All Execute bytes, including CR, call become_tentative(). The correct statement is: all CSI sequences except CSI 'C' (right arrow, 0x43) and CSI 'D' (left arrow, 0x44) call become_tentative(); all Esc_Dispatch actions call become_tentative(); all Execute bytes (including CR) call become_tentative(); and Print actions in the last column also call become_tentative(). CR is not excluded.

INACCURACY — SRTT trigger uses send_interval not raw SRTT: The survey states 'when SRTT > 80 ms, all unconfirmed predictions are underlined.' The cull() function (terminaloverlay.cc:434-446) gates on send_interval (the computed frame interval = ceil(SRTT/2) clamped to [20,250] ms), not on raw SRTT. The underline trigger fires when send_interval > FLAG_TRIGGER_HIGH (80 ms), which corresponds to SRTT > 160 ms, not SRTT > 80 ms. Similarly the SRTT_TRIGGER_HIGH (30 ms) corresponds to a raw SRTT of approximately 60 ms. The source comment says 'Try to send roughly two frames per RTT, bounded by limits on frame rate' (transportsender-impl.h:59).

MINOR IMPRECISION — ET reconnect direction: The survey says 'BackedReader sends its last_sequence_number and BackedWriter replays only the missing tail.' More precisely, BackedReader::getSequenceNumber() returns the count of packets successfully consumed by the reader; this value is passed to BackedWriter::recover(lastValidSequenceNumber) which uses it to determine which packets in the backupBuffer deque need to be reserialized and re-sent. The BackedReader does not actively 'send' anything — it is the client that reads BackedReader::getSequenceNumber() and transmits that value to the server as part of the reconnect handshake.
verifiedFacts: SRTT thresholds confirmed exactly from terminaloverlay.h:215-223: SRTT_TRIGGER_LOW=20, SRTT_TRIGGER_HIGH=30, FLAG_TRIGGER_LOW=50, FLAG_TRIGGER_HIGH=80, GLITCH_THRESHOLD=250, GLITCH_REPAIR_COUNT=10, GLITCH_REPAIR_MININTERVAL=150, GLITCH_FLAG_THRESHOLD=5000 — all in milliseconds.
SEND_MINDELAY=8 ms confirmed from transportsender-impl.h:55 constructor initializer.
send_interval formula confirmed: ceil(SRTT/2.0) clamped to [SEND_INTERVAL_MIN=20, SEND_INTERVAL_MAX=250] ms (transportsender-impl.h:63-70, transportsender.h:49-50).
Experimental mode confirmed: new_user_byte() sets prediction_epoch = confirmed_epoch at line 627 of terminaloverlay.cc, making all subsequent predictions immediately self-confirmed.
DisplayPreference enum confirmed with four values: Always, Never, Adaptive, Experimental (terminaloverlay.h:269-275). Default is Adaptive (constructor at line 311).
CSI prediction: only 'C' (right arrow) and 'D' (left arrow) proceed predictively; all other CSI dispatch calls become_tentative() (terminaloverlay.cc:808-825).
BackedWriter MAX_BACKUP_BYTES = 64 MB confirmed (BackedWriter.hpp:32). BackedWriter::recover(lastValidSequenceNumber) replays from the deque. BackedReader tracks sequenceNumber of consumed packets.

ET SSH bootstrap confirmed: SshSetupHandler.cpp uses subprocess ssh to launch etterminal on the server, passing a 32-char random passkey via stdin; etterminal prints IDPASSKEY: back to the client; all subsequent data flows over ET's own TCP connection to port 2022.

ghostty_surface_t is typedef void* — confirmed opaque. No cell-read function exists in include/ghostty.h. ghostty Terminal.zig and Screen.zig contain full internal cell state (Screen.Cursor, PageList cells) but none is exposed through the C embedding API.
WezTerm password heuristic confirmed: renderable.rs:158 checks line.as_str().contains("sword") — the substring 'sword' (present in 'password') gates prediction suppression for the whole line.
WezTerm local echo uses Underline::Double for predicted characters (renderable.rs:196), matching the survey's claim of 'double-underline styling.'

WezTerm should_predict() gates on local_echo_threshold_ms: self.last_input_rtt >= thresh (renderable.rs:139-143). last_input_rtt is computed from delta.input_serial.elapsed_millis() in apply_changes_to_surface (renderable.rs:332-334). This confirms the InputSerial RTT measurement mechanism.
pitfalls: SHADOW PARSER MAINTENANCE BURDEN: The shadow VT parser must handle the full VT/ANSI byte stream that libghostty handles, including multi-byte UTF-8 sequences, OSC sequences (window title etc.), and application-mode sequences. Any parser that is less complete than libghostty's internal parser will desync the shadow framebuffer from the real framebuffer, causing spurious mispredictions and jarring snaps. The parser must at minimum handle: UTF-8 multi-byte characters, CSI cursor motion (for become_tentative decisions), and SGR attributes (for accurate cell replacement content). A known-good starting point is Mosh's own Parser::UTF8Parser.

FULL-SCREEN APP DISABLEMENT IS CORRECT BUT MUST BE HANDLED: The PredictionEngine correctly disables prediction inside vim, emacs, and htop (all applications that use CSI cursor-positioning sequences). This is the right behavior, not a bug. Claude Code itself uses a TUI with cursor-positioning sequences, which means speculative echo will be OFF while the Claude Code TUI is active. Prediction will be ON for the raw shell prompt between Claude Code invocations. This is acceptable UX for the stated use case.

ALWAYS MODE AND PASSWORD PROMPTS: With display_preference=Always, predictions will be shown even at password prompts (sudo, SSH passphrase). The underline styling distinguishes predicted from confirmed characters, but the predicted character will be briefly visible before being suppressed by the server sending back an empty echo. For a private-use coding tool this is acceptable; if shared or screencasted, consider the WezTerm 'sword' heuristic as an additional gate.

SHADOW PARSER / LIBGHOSTTY SYNC: If the shadow parser processes bytes faster or slower than libghostty renders them (due to batching or buffering), the shadow framebuffer may be ahead of or behind the visible state. Predictions applied to the shadow must also result in an immediate libghostty re-render signal, otherwise the user sees no visual change. The mechanism to trigger a re-render from the client side must be verified against the libghostty embedded API (ghostty_surface_draw or equivalent). The current C API has ghostty_surface_draw() callable by the host app, but whether it can be driven from a background TCP receive thread is a threading constraint to verify.

TCP HEAD-OF-LINE BLOCKING IS REAL BUT MINOR AT 5-20 MS RTT: On WireGuard/NetBird at 5-20 ms RTT with near-zero packet loss, a TCP retransmit adds one additional RTT (~10-40 ms worst case). For Claude Code output bursts (long AI completions scrolling), this is imperceptible. For real-time keystroke echo the prediction mechanism eliminates the concern entirely. No mitigation needed.

NO CONFLICT WITH CURRENT ARCHITECTURE: The recommended mechanism — raw TCP byte stream + shadow parser + PredictionEngine overlay + ET-style reconnect buffer — is fully compatible with all stated decisions: no app-layer encryption (WireGuard handles it), plain TCP, libghostty for rendering, NetBird P2P. The shadow parser adds no network overhead and approximately 15-50 KB of client-side RAM.

PATH 2 CGEvent INJECTION RISK IS UNADDRESSED BY THIS RESEARCH FAMILY: The terminal protocol survey contains no findings relevant to CGEvent injection for remote GUI input. This remains the single highest implementation risk for PATH 2 and must be researched separately.
families.conflictsWithCurrent: No conflicts. The recommended mechanism — Mosh PredictionEngine over a shadow VT framebuffer, raw TCP transport, ET-style sequence-numbered reconnect buffer, dual TCP channels (data + control) — is fully compatible with all current architectural decisions: no app-layer encryption (WireGuard handles it), plain TCP for PATH 1, plain UDP for PATH 2, libghostty as the sole renderer, fps cap of 24-30 on PATH 2, NetBird P2P mesh. The shadow parser runs entirely client-side with no server changes required. The only forward dependency is on libghostty's threading model for driving re-renders from a background receive thread — this needs to be verified against the ghostty embedded API before implementation.

families.family: iOS / iPadOS terminal + remote-shell clients that feel native
families.mechanism: libghostty external-IO backend (use_custom_io = true + ghostty_surface_feed_data / ghostty_surface_set_write_callback) wired to a plain TCP PTY stream
families.recommendationPath1:
Adopt the libghostty external-IO pattern exactly as implemented in VVTerm and Geistty, both of which compile a patched fork of Ghostty (not upstream):

1. Build GhosttyKit.xcframework from the wiedymi/ghostty fork (branch "custom-io") or the daiimus/ghostty fork (branch "ios-external-backend"). Upstream ghostty HEAD does NOT expose ghostty_surface_feed_data or ghostty_surface_set_write_callback in its public include/ghostty.h — those are only in the forks' vendored headers.

2. Surface config: set surfaceConfig.use_custom_io = true. This switches the termio backend from Exec.zig (PTY) to External.zig, so no local shell is spawned.

3. Data path IN (host PTY → client display): when bytes arrive over the TCP connection, call ghostty_surface_feed_data(surface, ptr, len) on the main thread (@MainActor), then call ghostty_surface_refresh + ghostty_surface_draw via a coalescing DispatchQueue.main.async guard (the scheduleCustomIORedraw pattern from VVTerm GhosttyTerminalView+iOS.swift:885-900).

4. Data path OUT (keypress → host PTY): install a C write callback via ghostty_surface_set_write_callback(surface, callback, userdata). The callback fires synchronously on the main thread from inside Ghostty's key encoder. In the callback, schedule a Task(priority: .userInitiated) to write the bytes to the TCP connection (VVTerm SSHTerminalWrapper.swift:127).

5. Key repeat: UIKit delivers pressesBegan once and pressesEnded once; it does NOT repeat. Implement a DispatchSourceTimer with deadline .now() + 0.35, repeating 0.05 (350 ms initial delay, 50 ms = 20 Hz repeat rate). Fire GHOSTTY_ACTION_REPEAT key events to ghostty_surface_key() on each tick. Cancel the timer in pressesEnded. This is verified in VVTerm GhosttyTerminalView+iOS.swift:3101.

6. IME proxy: deploy a hidden @MainActor UITextView as the UITextInput host (VVTerm TerminalIMEProxyTextView pattern). Route hardware Ctrl+letter and Alt+letter directly to ghostty_surface_key(); route all other text through IME proxy → ghostty_surface_text(). Never implement UITextInput on the same UIView that handles pressesBegan.

7. iOS backgrounding: call UIApplication.beginBackgroundTask in the UISceneDidEnterBackgroundNotification handler (verified in Blink AppDelegate.m:115, 251). Schedule a timer for MIN(backgroundTimeRemaining * 0.9, 300s) to cleanly close or suspend the TCP connection before iOS freezes the process. On foreground, reconnect to the PTY server-side multiplexer (tmux is the correct answer here — see PATH 1 note below).

8. Floating cursor: implement UITextInput.updateFloatingCursor(at:) to map horizontal drag > 5pt threshold to left/right arrow sequences, and vertical drag (alternate screen only) to up/down sequences — verified verbatim in SwiftTerm iOSTextInput.swift:462-507. This is the single most important iOS-specific UX fix for coding use.

9. On-screen accessory bar: always show Ctrl, Esc, Tab, and arrow keys when the software keyboard is visible. This is table stakes confirmed by Blink, Geistty, VVTerm, and SwiftTerm.

PATH 1 specific note: because TCP connections die during iOS sleep, run tmux on the server side and reconnect to the existing session on foreground. This gives the same session-persistence benefit as Mosh's state serialization without adopting Mosh's UDP transport or prediction engine. Do NOT implement Mosh local-echo prediction on top of a 5-20ms WireGuard mesh — the adaptive mode threshold means prediction would be suppressed anyway (predictions are withheld on fast links), and the complexity cost is not justified for a sub-20ms path.
families.recommendationPath2:
PATH 2 (ScreenCaptureKit / HEVC / UDP) is a GUI video streaming path. None of the iOS terminal clients in this family implement or inform it — they are all terminal-only. The mechanism above (libghostty external IO) does not apply to PATH 2.

For PATH 2, the single actionable lesson from this family is negative: do not try to unify the terminal renderer with the video path. libghostty renders only its own cell grid via Metal/IOSurface. Per-window capture (ScreenCaptureKit SCWindowStream) must be a separate pipeline from the terminal surface. The two paths run in parallel: PATH 1 for terminal/TUI windows, PATH 2 for GUI windows. No mechanism from the iOS terminal client family transfers to PATH 2's capture-encode-transmit-decode loop.

corrections: INACCURACY: The survey states mosh state is serialised via NSKeyedArchiver into 'protected storage'. Primary source (MoshSession.m:605-608) shows it is stored via sessionParams.putEncodedState(), which invokes the BKSessionParamsSnapshotting protocol (NSSecureCoding-conforming). NSKeyedArchiver may be used internally by the concrete BKSessionParams implementation, but the direct storage mechanism is the protocol method, not NSKeyedArchiver directly. The 'protected storage' claim has no basis in the code examined.

INACCURACY: The survey states VVTerm uses 'libssh2/OpenSSL' as SSH transport and describes the SSHClient actor wrapping a MoshClientSession. VVTerm does use libssh2, but the survey's description of SSHClient as an 'actor' is misleading — the libssh2 session is a class-based wrapper around an OpaquePointer (libssh2Session), not a Swift actor. The 'MoshClientSession enqueue' path exists for Mosh mode but is a separate code path from the core SSH path.

INACCURACY: The survey states 'ghostty_surface_feed_data() called on main thread as SSH data arrives (shouldContinueStreaming closure)'. Primary source shows feedData in GhosttyTerminalView+iOS.swift:3773 is NOT inside a 'shouldContinueStreaming' closure — that closure name does not appear in the VVTerm codebase. The actual call site is at TerminalContainerView.swift:484 inside the @MainActor SSHSessionDelegate method sshSession(_:didReceiveData:).

INACCURACY: The survey states upstream libghostty is ready for PATH 1 use with ghostty_surface_feed_data and ghostty_surface_set_write_callback. These functions do NOT exist in upstream ghostty HEAD (verified by grepping include/ghostty.h which has 1208 lines but no feed_data or write_callback). Both VVTerm and Geistty use privately-forked and patched builds of Ghostty. Any project adopting this pattern must either use one of these forks or maintain its own patch.

INACCURACY: The survey describes Ghostty's parser as using 'SIMD-accelerated terminal sequence parsing' and 'vectorised byte scanning for escape sequence boundaries'. Primary source (src/terminal/stream.zig:519-523) shows SIMD is used specifically for UTF-8 decoding in the ground state (simd.vt.utf8DecodeUntilControlSeq), not for general escape sequence boundary detection. The state machine itself is a standard table-driven parser; SIMD accelerates the ASCII/UTF-8 fast path within the ground state only.

INACCURACY: The survey states VVTerm fires 'ghostty_surface_key() with GHOSTTY_ACTION_REPEAT'. Primary source (GhosttyTerminalView+iOS.swift:3109-3124) shows the timer fires sendDirectHardwareKeyEvent with action GHOSTTY_ACTION_REPEAT, and if that fails, falls back to surface.sendKeyEvent with action .repeat. The .repeat action is a Swift enum value that maps to Ghostty's repeat action — it is not GHOSTTY_ACTION_REPEAT called directly on ghostty_surface_key.

INACCURACY: The survey states SwiftTerm's floating cursor maps 'vertical drag to up/down in alternate screen mode'. The primary source (iOSTextInput.swift:487-505) shows vertical movement is gated on terminal.isCurrentBufferAlternate — meaning it only works when the alternate screen buffer is active (e.g., vim, less), not in the normal scrollback buffer. The survey correctly notes this condition but frames it as a feature for 'alternate screen mode' without clarifying it is disabled in the normal screen (the scrollback case), which is the state most users would expect cursor movement to work.
pitfalls: ghostty_surface_feed_data and ghostty_surface_set_write_callback are not in upstream Ghostty. You must track and maintain a fork (wiedymi/ghostty or daiimus/ghostty) or submit PRs upstream. The daiimus fork also adds ~3100 lines of tmux control mode machinery (viewer.zig, modified stream_handler.zig) that you will carry as dead weight if you use tmux on the server side without Ghostty's built-in tmux parser.

Key repeat MUST be a DispatchSourceTimer in your own code. UIKit fires pressesBegan exactly once per key-down and pressesEnded once per key-up. If you skip the 350ms/50ms timer, held arrow keys and held Delete will not repeat — breaking the single most common navigation pattern in a coding terminal.

The IME proxy must be a separate UITextView from the terminal surface UIView. If you implement UITextInput on the Metal view that also handles pressesBegan, iOS will deliver marked-text events and raw key events through the same responder chain with unpredictable ordering. CJK input will be broken. This is not a 'nice to have' — Claude Code has Japanese users.

iOS kills TCP connections within seconds of backgrounding without a UIBackgroundTask. The correct mitigation is server-side tmux: connect to a named tmux session on launch, detach on background, re-attach on foreground. This requires the host to run tmux. If the host does not run tmux, the client must use UIApplication.beginBackgroundTask and write bytes to the TCP connection at least every ~25 seconds (iOS background execution limit is 30s in practice, but varies by device and battery state).

The floating-cursor feature (two-finger drag on software keyboard) is gated on Apple's UITextInput protocol. If your terminal view does not implement UITextInput correctly, iOS will not call updateFloatingCursor(at:) and users on iPhone will have no practical way to move the cursor without a hardware keyboard. Implement the full UITextInput surface area, not just insertText/deleteBackward.

Dead-key IME guard (Blink's _imeGuardDown/_imeGuardUp for alt+e/u/i/n) is only needed when users type on hardware keyboards with non-US layouts (e.g., French AZERTY, German QWERTZ). On a US keyboard, these combos work correctly. If your target user base is exclusively US-layout users, you can omit this — but if any international keyboard is supported, the guard is required or alt+e will compose an acute accent instead of emitting ESC+e.

libghostty builds for iOS require Zig installed at build time and Apple Silicon. The VVTerm build script shells out to Zig to compile Ghostty, then packages the result as a .xcframework. This adds a non-trivial build-time dependency. Budget the build infrastructure cost before committing to the libghostty path.

The on-screen accessory bar (Ctrl, Esc, Tab, arrow keys) must be shown only when the software keyboard is visible. If it appears with a hardware keyboard, it wastes screen space. Detect hardware keyboard presence via keyboard frame height < 150pt (verified from Blink KBTracker.swift:55) or via UIKeyboardWillShowNotification frame height.
families.conflictsWithCurrent: No conflicts with declared decisions. The mechanism (libghostty custom IO over plain TCP) is exactly the architecture you have already chosen. Specific compatibility notes: (1) No app-layer encryption: ghostty_surface_feed_data receives raw bytes; encryption/decryption happens at the WireGuard layer before bytes reach the app, which is correct and requires no change. (2) Plain TCP for terminal: the write callback delivers bytes that you write directly to the TCP socket — no additional framing or encryption needed. (3) libghostty-only renderer: the external-IO backend is the intended embedding pattern; the upstream maintainer explicitly designed use_custom_io for this purpose. (4) fps cap 24-30 for PATH 2: irrelevant to PATH 1 (terminal has no frame rate concept; redraw is event-driven via scheduleCustomIORedraw). (5) NetBird P2P 5-20ms RTT: at this latency, Mosh's adaptive prediction is suppressed (predictions are withheld on fast links), confirming that the local-echo prediction system is unnecessary for your network — the WireGuard mesh effectively eliminates the problem Mosh solves.

families.family: commercial low-latency remote desktop / app streaming
families.mechanism: VideoToolbox EnableLowLatencyRateControl + AllowFrameReordering=false (parallel capture/encode threads, critical OS priority, do NOT set max_ref_frames=1 on H.264)
families.recommendationPath1: PATH 1 (libghostty / TCP / PTY) — this mechanism does not apply. The terminal path streams raw VT bytes over plain TCP; there is no video encoder in the loop. The PTY byte-stream design already eliminates protocol round-trips by construction (confirmed by the NX precedent). No action needed on the encoder side for PATH 1. The one relevant lesson from the survey for PATH 1 is from NX: the PTY-over-TCP design is already the correct architecture — do not add buffering layers between posix_spawn and the TCP write. Write PTY output bytes to the TCP socket on the same thread or with a minimal lockless relay; do not pass through a ring buffer that adds latency. Elevate the PTY-read-and-forward thread to QOS_CLASS_USER_INTERACTIVE.
families.recommendationPath2:
PATH 2 (ScreenCaptureKit / HEVC / UDP) — implement all of the following as a single coherent pipeline:

1. SESSION CREATION: When creating the VTCompressionSession for HEVC on Apple Silicon, set kVTVideoEncoderSpecification_EnableLowLatencyRateControl = kCFBooleanTrue in the encoder specification dictionary. This is confirmed valid for HEVC on Apple Silicon (ARM64) by the FFmpeg videotoolboxenc.c commit (line 1715: `TARGET_CPU_ARM64 && avctx->codec_id == AV_CODEC_ID_HEVC`). On Intel Macs, EnableLowLatencyRateControl is H.264-only — but this project targets Apple Silicon hosts exclusively, so HEVC low-latency mode is available. Also set kVTCompressionPropertyKey_AllowFrameReordering = kCFBooleanFalse explicitly as a belt-and-suspenders measure.

2. DO NOT SET max_ref_frames=1 FOR H.264: Verified in Sunshine src/video.cpp lines 1136-1141 (and confirmed by GitHub issue #5013): setting ReferenceBufferCount=1 on Apple Silicon VideoToolbox H.264 causes every frame to be an IDR — P-frames are never generated, bandwidth inflates ~3x. HEVC and AV1 are unaffected. For HEVC (your chosen codec), max_ref_frames=1 is safe and is retained by Sunshine. Do not apply this restriction to H.264 if you ever need to fall back.

3. PARALLEL THREADS WITH CRITICAL PRIORITY: Run two threads — (a) SCStream output handler / capture thread, (b) VideoToolbox encode thread — decoupled by a lock-free single-frame queue. Verified in Sunshine src/video.cpp line 1396: `platf::adjust_thread_priority(platf::thread_priority_e::critical)` on the capture thread. The encode thread should run at the same critical priority. Both must drain immediately — no intermediate ring buffers.

4. SCStream queueDepth: Sunshine uses queueDepth=5 (sc_capture.m line 204). Set to 3 (the documented minimum) to reduce capture-to-encode pipeline depth. Each extra buffer slot is one frame-interval of potential latency.

5. CURSOR CHANNEL (separate UDP datagram): Implement a dedicated out-of-band UDP send for cursor position + rendered cursor image, completely decoupled from the HEVC video stream. This is confirmed by Parsec patent US 9798436 (not US 10951890 as cited in survey — see corrections) and the Parsec technology page description of cursor/video separation. The client renders the cursor locally using the received position and image; cursor latency equals network RTT regardless of encode/decode pipeline depth. This is the single highest-impact native-feel mechanism for GUI use.

6. LTR FRAMES FOR LOSS RECOVERY: Enable kVTCompressionPropertyKey_EnableLTR. On packet loss, send kVTEncodeFrameOptionKey_ForceLTRRefresh instead of forcing an IDR. WWDC 2021 confirms this eliminates the bandwidth spike associated with keyframe recovery. This requires application-layer ACKs from the client back to the host (the client must report which LTR frame tokens were received).

7. IDLE FRAME SKIP: ScreenCaptureKit delivers frames only when content changes — honor this by skipping encode when the CVPixelBuffer is null or the SCStream output callback indicates no dirty region. Sunshine sc_capture.m re-delivers the last valid frame on null callbacks (lines 269-277); for a per-window capture targeting an SCContentFilter(window:), suppress re-transmission entirely and send a heartbeat IDR every ~1s instead.
verifiedFacts: VideoToolbox kVTVideoEncoderSpecification_EnableLowLatencyRateControl is valid for HEVC on Apple Silicon (ARM64). FFmpeg videotoolboxenc.c line 1715 explicitly gates HEVC low-latency mode on TARGET_CPU_ARM64: `(avctx->codec_id == AV_CODEC_ID_H264) || (TARGET_CPU_ARM64 && avctx->codec_id == AV_CODEC_ID_HEVC)`. The WWDC 2021 session stated H.264-only, but the FFmpeg commit post-dates that and reflects Apple's extended support for HEVC on Apple Silicon.

The max_ref_frames=1 / all-IDR bug is real and confirmed: Sunshine src/video.cpp lines 1136-1141 carry the comment 'max_ref_frames is intentionally omitted for H.264 because VideoToolbox on Apple Silicon produces all-IDR output when ReferenceBufferCount=1 is set for H.264, causing massive bandwidth inflation (~3x) and frame drops. HEVC and AV1 are unaffected and retain max_ref_frames=1. See LizardByte/Sunshine#5013.' Issue #5013 independently confirms the behavior via user report.

Sunshine video capture thread is set to critical OS priority at video.cpp line 1396: `platf::adjust_thread_priority(platf::thread_priority_e::critical)`. Audio capture is also critical (audio.cpp line 208). Audio encode is elevated to 'high' (audio.cpp line 95). This confirms the survey claim about thread priority elevation.

Sunshine's parallel capture/encode architecture is confirmed: `start_capture_async` (video.cpp line 3109) launches a dedicated `captureThread` as a separate std::thread, decoupled from encode sessions via a queue (`safe::queue_t<capture_ctx_t>`).

Sunshine's SCStream implementation (sc_capture.m line 204) sets queueDepth=5, not 3. The survey claim that 'SCStream queueDepth=3 (minimum)' is the implemented value is incorrect for Sunshine — it uses 5.

CGEventPost requires Accessibility permission on macOS. Multi.app blog confirms: 'your app will need to have Accessibility permission granted from the user for this to work.' CGWarpMouseCursorPosition bypasses CGEvent taps entirely — confirmed by the same source.

Parsec's cursor separation is confirmed as a real architectural feature by patent US 9798436 (not US 10951890). The Parsec technology page confirms video/audio/cursor/input as separate stream components over BUD protocol.

WWDC 2021 VideoToolbox session confirms low-latency rate control reduces latency by up to 100ms for 720p 30fps video by eliminating frame reordering (one-in-one-out) and enabling faster rate controller adaptation. Always uses hardware-accelerated encoder path.
corrections: CORRECTION — EnableLowLatencyRateControl codec scope: The survey states 'H.264 only as of iOS 15 / macOS 12; for HEVC set kVTCompressionPropertyKey_AllowFrameReordering=false explicitly.' This is outdated. On Apple Silicon (ARM64), kVTVideoEncoderSpecification_EnableLowLatencyRateControl is also valid for HEVC, as confirmed by FFmpeg videotoolboxenc.c line 1715. Since this project targets Apple Silicon hosts, HEVC gets full low-latency rate control, not just AllowFrameReordering=false. Both properties should still be set, but EnableLowLatencyRateControl does apply to HEVC on the target platform.

CORRECTION — Parsec cursor patent number: The survey cites US 10,951,890 as the 'separate cursor channel' patent. The patent actually verified via USPTO to be the low-latency mouse mode / asynchronous cursor channel patent is US 9,798,436 ('Remote computing with a low latency mouse mode'). Patent 10,951,890 was inaccessible (scanned image PDF, no OCR). The cursor channel architecture is real and confirmed, but the primary citation should reference US 9,798,436.

CORRECTION — Sunshine SCStream queueDepth is 5, not 3: The survey's cross-cutting lesson states 'SCStreamConfiguration queueDepth: set to 3 (minimum valid).' Sunshine's sc_capture.m sets queueDepth=5. Apple's documentation does not specify 3 as the minimum in any publicly accessible form — the '3 is minimum' claim is unverified. Use the lowest value that does not cause SCStream to error; empirically Sunshine uses 5 and this should be treated as the proven safe value until Apple clarifies the minimum.

CORRECTION — max_ref_frames=1 bug scope: The survey says 'Do not set max_ref_frames=1 on VideoToolbox H.264.' This is correct for H.264. However, the survey implies the fix was made in 'Lumen fork' — the fix is now present in upstream Sunshine src/video.cpp (lines 1136-1141 with explicit comment referencing issue #5013). The Lumen fork was the first to identify and implement this fix, but it is no longer Lumen-exclusive.

CORRECTION — Parsec BUD 'four parallel connections' claim: The survey states 'Four parallel connections: video, audio, cursor, and user-input.' This specific '4 connections' architecture is NOT documented in any publicly accessible Parsec blog post or support page. The Parsec technology page confirms video/audio/cursor/input as stream components but does not specify whether each runs on a separate UDP socket or is multiplexed on one. The architectural principle (cursor decoupled from video) is confirmed; the '4 separate UDP connections' detail is not verifiable from public sources and may be a reasonable inference rather than a documented fact.
pitfalls: EnableLowLatencyRateControl requires an explicit bitrate when used with HEVC. FFmpeg videotoolboxenc.c line 1716-1720 shows the encoder returns AVERROR(EINVAL) if bit_rate is 0 in low-delay mode. Set a target bitrate explicitly — do not rely on automatic/CQ mode when this flag is enabled.

kVTCompressionPropertyKey_UsingHardwareAcceleratedVideoEncoder query returns -12900 (kVTPropertyNotSupportedErr) when EnableLowLatencyRateControl is active, per Apple Developer Forums. Do not use this property to verify hardware acceleration is active when low-latency mode is enabled — it will always error. Verify hardware acceleration separately at session creation without the low-latency flag.

CGEventPost with kCGHIDEventTap requires the Accessibility entitlement (com.apple.security.automation.apple-events is insufficient; the actual Privacy & Security > Accessibility toggle must be granted by the user). This means the PATH 2 host application cannot be sandboxed and cannot be distributed via the Mac App Store. This is the primary distribution constraint for PATH 2 input injection.

Cursor channel via CGWarpMouseCursorPosition bypasses CGEvent taps but also bypasses the application-level event loop. If both host and client run on the same machine (development scenario), using CGWarpMouseCursorPosition for remote cursor updates while also having a local CGEvent tap can cause cursor position fights. Use eventSourceUserData tagging on all CGEventPost calls to allow the host's own tap to filter out remote-injected events.

LTR frame loss recovery (kVTCompressionPropertyKey_EnableLTR) requires client-to-host ACK messaging. This adds a control feedback path (small UDP message client→host per received LTR frame). On a 5-20ms RTT WireGuard mesh this is cheap, but the implementation must handle ACK loss — use a timeout-based fallback IDR if no ACK is received within 2 RTTs.

FPS cap of 24-30 (your stated constraint) means each frame slot is 33-42ms wide. With HEVC encode at ~18ms on M4 plus ~10ms WireGuard RTT plus ~2ms hardware decode, the total pipeline is ~30ms — just within a single frame at 30fps but with minimal slack. Any encode jitter that exceeds ~12ms will cause a frame to be late. Elevating both threads to critical priority and eliminating queues (as described) is mandatory to stay within this budget, not optional.

SCContentFilter(window:) for per-window capture is not the same as capturing a virtual display. Window captures include only the window's content region — if the window is occluded by another window, the occluded portion is captured as black or with compositor-composited content depending on macOS version. On macOS 14+, SCStream with SCContentFilter for a specific window captures the window's backing store (not the composited screen), which gives correct content regardless of occlusion. Verify this behavior on the target macOS version.

Transport encryption redundancy confirmed: WireGuard already provides ChaCha20-Poly1305 authenticated encryption per packet. Adding any inner encryption layer (DTLS, TLS, AES-CBC) doubles crypto overhead with no security benefit. Use plain UDP with a 4-byte sequence number and optional CRC32C for ordering and loss detection only.
families.conflictsWithCurrent: NO direct conflicts. All recommendations align with the stated architecture. Specific alignment points: (1) Plain UDP for video — confirmed correct; skip QUIC/DTLS entirely inside WireGuard. (2) HEVC on Apple Silicon — EnableLowLatencyRateControl now confirmed valid for HEVC on ARM64, not H.264-only as the survey implied. (3) libghostty for terminal — no encoder involvement; PTY byte-stream path unaffected by all of the above. (4) fps cap 24-30 — tightens the latency budget but is achievable with the pipeline described (30ms total vs 33ms frame slot at 30fps). (5) NetBird P2P at 5-20ms RTT — LTR ACK round-trips are cheap at this RTT. One advisory: the survey describes the terminal path as 'plain TCP.' For a coding use-case (interactive keystrokes at 1-10 byte/event), TCP's Nagle algorithm will buffer small writes and add up to 200ms of latency. Disable Nagle (TCP_NODELAY) on the socket immediately after connect. This is not a conflict with current decisions but is a mandatory implementation detail the survey does not mention.

families.family: open-source low-latency remote desktop / game streaming
families.mechanism: Client-side cursor rendering: send input as absolute coordinates, hide the host-rendered cursor, display the cursor locally on the client. This eliminates all perceived pointer latency regardless of network RTT — the single highest-impact "feels native" technique in the entire survey.
families.recommendationPath1: PATH 1 (libghostty / TCP / PTY): Client-side cursor rendering does not apply — there is no cursor embedded in a VT byte stream. The correct mechanism for PATH 1 is unsolicited server-push over plain TCP with no application-layer flow control: write PTY output bytes to the socket immediately as they arrive from the master FD; the client renders with libghostty which handles its own display pipeline. No FEC, no RTP, no pacing needed. The only latency floor is network RTT (5–20 ms on the NetBird mesh). Do NOT add a request-response ping-pong: the server writes, the client renders. Input (raw bytes) goes back over the same TCP connection to PTY stdin. This matches the TigerVNC "unsolicited update" lesson: the RFB request-response model adds one RTT per screen update; eliminating it halves perceived latency for interactive use.
families.recommendationPath2: PATH 2 (ScreenCaptureKit / HEVC / UDP): Implement client-side cursor rendering as the first priority. Concretely: (a) on the host, call CGDisplayHideCursor() or use ScreenCaptureKit SCStreamConfiguration.showsCursor = false to exclude the cursor from captured frames; (b) separately sample NSEvent mouse position at ~120 Hz and send cursor position + shape (NSCursor.currentSystem image + hotspot) over a lightweight UDP or TCP side channel — NOT embedded in the HEVC bitstream; (c) on the client, composite a CALayer or Metal-rendered cursor quad over the decoded video frame before display. This decouples pointer feedback from the encode/decode pipeline entirely. The second priority is setting AV_CODEC_FLAG_LOW_DELAY equivalent on the VideoToolbox decode session: set kVTDecompressionPropertyKey_RealTime = true and do not use B-frames in the HEVC encode (VideoToolbox HEVC encoder: set kVTCompressionPropertyKey_MaxKeyFrameIntervalDuration to your GOP length and ensure B-frames are off via kVTCompressionPropertyKey_AllowFrameReordering = false). This keeps the decode pipeline at single-frame latency with no reorder buffer. Third priority: encode in constant-quality mode (kVTCompressionPropertyKey_Quality) rather than CBR so idle frames are tiny, and use ScreenCaptureKit's minimumFrameInterval to skip encoding when the window has not changed.
verifiedFacts: FEC default of 20%: confirmed in Sunshine src_assets/common/assets/web/config.html line 275 ("fec_percentage": 20) and docs/configuration.md line 1988 (fec_percentage = 20). The fec::encode() function in src/stream.cpp lines 611–718 implements it as (data_shards * fecpercentage + 99) / 100 parity shards, computed per-frame, with a configurable minparityshards floor.

RFI dedicated thread: confirmed — PLT_THREAD invalidateRefFramesThread named 'InvRefFrames' created at moonlight-common-c/src/ControlStream.c line 2032, running referenceFrameControlFunc() at line 1582. The queue (referenceFrameControlQueue, capacity 20) is populated by queueFrameInvalidationTuple() and drained by this thread. Separate from requestIdrFrameThread.

Speculative RFI mechanism confirmed but mis-described in survey: the actual trigger is a shard-count comparison in reconstructFrame() — when missingPackets > totalPackets - neededPackets AND receivedOosData == false, notifyFrameLost(frameNumber, true) fires immediately (before the next frame arrives), sending an RFI before the decode deadline. It is NOT based on 'packet arrival stats' or timing. The OOS-data flag has a 5-minute cooldown (SPECULATIVE_RFI_COOLDOWN_PERIOD_US = 300000000 µs). If speculative RFI misfires, the mode is suppressed for 5 minutes.

AV_CODEC_FLAG_LOW_DELAY and AV_CODEC_FLAG_OUTPUT_CORRUPT confirmed in moonlight-qt/app/streaming/video/ffmpeg.cpp completeInitialization(): m_VideoDecoderCtx->flags |= AV_CODEC_FLAG_LOW_DELAY; m_VideoDecoderCtx->flags |= AV_CODEC_FLAG_OUTPUT_CORRUPT; m_VideoDecoderCtx->flags2 |= AV_CODEC_FLAG2_SHOW_ALL. All three set unconditionally.
KeyComboToggleCursorHide bound to SDLK_c at moonlight-qt/app/streaming/input/input.cpp line 94. All special combos use CTRL+ALT+SHIFT, so CTRL+ALT+SHIFT+C is correct.

Mouse motion batching confirmed: moonlight-qt/app/streaming/input/mouse.cpp lines 87–98 drain all pending SDL_MOUSEMOTION events with SDL_PeepEvents() accumulating xrel/yrel before a single LiSendMouseMoveEvent() call.

ENet control channel confirmed: moonlight-common-c/src/ControlStream.c uses ENetHost* client and ENetPeer* peer for control traffic. sendMessageEnet() is used for IDR requests, RFI tuples, and input data (IDX_INPUT_DATA = 5 on Gen5+).
corrections: Survey claim: 'Speculative RFI: client predicts whether a frame will be unrecoverable based on packet arrival stats.' CORRECTION: The actual trigger is a mathematical shard-count check in reconstructFrame() (RtpVideoQueue.c). When the number of missing shards exceeds available parity shards AND the host has not recently sent out-of-order packets (receivedOosData == false), the client fires notifyFrameLost() speculatively, before the frame deadline. The phrase 'packet arrival stats' is misleading — it is a deterministic parity algebra comparison, not a statistical model of arrival timing.

Survey claim: 'Client sends IDR request; server selectively invalidates only the lost reference frames rather than forcing full IDR.' CORRECTION: The direction of invalidation is from client to server. The client sends an RFI range (startFrame, endFrame) via the control channel. The server (Sunshine) marks those reference frames as invalid and sends the next frame as if those frames do not exist as references, without generating a full IDR. The client's VideoDepacketizer uses dropFrameState() + connectionDetectedFrameLoss() to trigger this. This is correct at a high level but the survey omits that RFI only works when isReferenceFrameInvalidationEnabled() returns true — this requires server capability advertisement and is Sunshine-specific (IS_SUNSHINE() checks are present).

Survey claim for cursor: 'Client-side software cursor: user can hide host cursor and render local cursor client-side.' CORRECTION: In the current moonlight-qt SDL path, KeyComboToggleCursorHide toggles SDL_ShowCursor() for the local cursor visibility in absolute mouse mode. There is no separate cursor-shape-over-side-channel stream in moonlight-qt. The host cursor is hidden on the host side by platform display capture. The survey's description of 'send cursor shape/position metadata over a low-latency side channel' describes the Selkies pattern (cursor sent as JSON over WebRTC data channel), not what moonlight-qt actually implements. For our project, the Selkies pattern is the correct one to implement.

Survey claim: 'Sunshine src/config.cpp line 543' for FEC default. CORRECTION: The actual default value appears in src_assets/common/assets/web/config.html line 275 and docs/configuration.md line 1988. src/config.cpp does not have a line 543 with fec_percentage in the current codebase. The config.h line 181 declares the field; the default is set elsewhere.
pitfalls: Client-side cursor on macOS requires CGDisplayHideCursor() which takes a display ID argument — this hides the cursor system-wide on that display, not just in the streamed window. For per-window streaming (PATH 2 ScreenCaptureKit), use SCStreamConfiguration.showsCursor = false instead, which suppresses the cursor only in the captured stream without hiding it globally.

Cursor side-channel must be a separate socket/channel from the video UDP stream. If the cursor position update is sent over the same UDP path as HEVC frames (which are large and may queue), cursor updates will be delayed by video backpressure. Use a separate UDP socket with minimal packet size (<64 bytes: x, y, cursor ID, timestamp).
ScreenCaptureKit SCStreamConfiguration.showsCursor = false was introduced in macOS 12.3. If targeting macOS 12.0–12.2, fall back to CGDisplayHideCursor().

AV_CODEC_FLAG2_SHOW_ALL has a side effect: it outputs frames with missing reference data, which may manifest as green blocks or corruption artifacts on packet loss. On a WireGuard mesh this is acceptable (loss is near-zero) but requires the client Metal renderer to not treat corrupt frames as errors.

kVTDecompressionPropertyKey_RealTime on VideoToolbox does not guarantee single-frame decode latency on all macOS hardware — on Apple Silicon, the media engine pipeline may still buffer 2 frames. Verify with CMSampleBufferGetPresentationTimeStamp() vs wall clock on real hardware before assuming sub-16ms decode.

Speculative RFI is disabled for 5 minutes after any misprediction or OOS packet (SPECULATIVE_RFI_COOLDOWN_PERIOD_US = 300000000 µs). On a WireGuard mesh, OOS is essentially impossible, so speculative RFI will always be active — this is the happy path. But if you ever see speculative RFI fire incorrectly, it degrades to full IDR recovery for 5 minutes.

For PATH 2, CGEvent injection (the project's single biggest risk) is orthogonal to the cursor rendering mechanism. Even with perfect client-side cursor display, injected CGEvents must have the correct screen coordinates mapped from video-region coordinates — a mismatch here causes the cursor to visually appear in one location while clicks register elsewhere.
families.conflictsWithCurrent: No conflicts. The recommended mechanism (client-side cursor rendering) is additive to existing decisions: plain TCP for PATH 1 is unchanged; plain UDP for PATH 2 is unchanged; the cursor side-channel is a new lightweight parallel connection that adds no latency to the video pipeline. The showsCursor = false SCStreamConfiguration flag is compatible with ScreenCaptureKit per-window capture (SCWindow filter). FPS cap at 24–30 with idle-skip is unchanged. NetBird P2P assumption means the cursor side-channel UDP packet (< 64 bytes) arrives in < 5 ms — well under any perceptible threshold. No app-layer encryption is required on the cursor channel since WireGuard encrypts the tunnel.
