families.mechanism: Lossy-first then lossless-upgrade (auto-refresh pattern), combined with idle-frame skip (SCFrameStatus.idle gating). This is the single highest-value mechanism because it simultaneously solves the two most painful problems for a coding tool: (1) first-frame latency — a lossy HEVC frame is sent within one batch-delay interval (~5-20ms on LAN) so the user sees a response at network RTT speed, and (2) text legibility — a follow-up lossless re-encode of the same dirty rectangles is scheduled after a period of inactivity, ensuring that the code the user is actually reading is pixel-perfect. Without this pattern, the developer must choose between fast-but-blurry or correct-but-slow. The idle-skip component eliminates encode work on static content (>90% of frames during reading), which makes the encode queue vacant so the lossy first-frame can get a hardware slot immediately when the next keystroke arrives.
families.recommendationPath1: PATH 1 (libghostty / TCP / PTY) — this mechanism does not apply. The terminal path streams raw VT bytes over TCP; there is no video encoding stage, no IOSurface, and no quality/lossiness axis. libghostty renders text from VT codes at full fidelity by definition. The only latency technique that matters on PATH 1 is keeping the TCP write path as close to zero-copy as possible (write PTY output bytes directly to the TCP send buffer without intermediate copies) and ensuring the read loop on the client writes immediately to libghostty's input without buffering. No lossy-lossless pattern is relevant here.
families.recommendationPath2: PATH 2 (ScreenCaptureKit / HEVC / UDP) — implement as follows. (1) Idle-gate: in the SCStream didOutputSampleBuffer callback, read SCStreamFrameInfo.status; if it equals SCFrameStatus.idle, return immediately — do not enqueue anything for VideoToolbox or UDP. This is the zero-cost damage check equivalent to X11 DAMAGE DeltaRectangles. (2) On SCFrameStatus.complete, extract the IOSurface from the CMSampleBuffer, wrap it in a CVPixelBuffer via CVPixelBufferCreateWithIOSurface (zero CPU copy), and submit to VTCompressionSession with kVTCompressionPropertyKey_Quality set to 0.65 (lossy first-frame). Transmit the resulting NAL unit over UDP immediately. (3) Simultaneously, append the dirty region (from SCStreamFrameInfo.dirtyRects if available, otherwise the full window bounds) to a refresh_regions list. Start a GCD DispatchSourceTimer with a deadline of base_refresh_delay milliseconds (recommended starting value: max(batch_delay × 5, 200ms) — see correction below about the 1000ms claim). (4) If another SCFrameStatus.complete arrives before the timer fires, cancel and reschedule the timer (this mirrors Xpra's cancel_refresh_timer logic). (5) When the timer fires and the window has been idle for the full delay, re-encode the accumulated dirty union at kVTCompressionPropertyKey_Quality 1.0 (lossless HEVC) and transmit. (6) Window chrome regions (title bar, scroll bars, dock items) — add a LOSSLESS_WINDOW_TYPES equivalent: any SCWindow whose title matches a UI chrome heuristic, or whose pixel dimensions are narrow (height < 40px), should always use quality=1.0 even on the first encode, skipping the lossy step entirely. (7) Maintain one VTCompressionSession per tracked SCWindow (not per IOSurface or per CMSampleBuffer) to preserve encoder state across frames and avoid the Waypipe per-buffer flicker bug. (8) UDP queue discipline: cap the encode queue at 2 frames in flight; if a third lossy frame arrives before the first is ACKed, drop the oldest. Never backpressure the SCKit callback thread.
verifiedFacts: RAIL local move/resize (MS-RDPERP §1.3.2.5 confirmed): server sends Server Min Max Info PDU then Server Move/Size Start PDU; client injects mouse-down or posts window command; local WM handles all drag events; on mouse-up, client sends mouse-button-up to server; for keyboard moves and ALL resize operations, client also sends Client Window Move PDU. The survey says 'only the final position is sent back via Client Window Move PDU' — this is slightly imprecise: for mouse-based moves, no Client Window Move PDU is sent; the mouse-up is sufficient. For resize and keyboard moves, the PDU is required.

SCFrameStatus.idle confirmed (WWDC 2022 session 10156): 'An idle frame status means the video sample hasn't changed, so there is no new IOSurface.' The sample buffer is delivered but carries no new surface — the survey's claim that the IOSurface is absent on idle is correct.
Xpra INITIAL_QUALITY confirmed as 65 (compress.py line: INITIAL_QUALITY = envint('XPRA_INITIAL_QUALITY', 65)).
Xpra LOSSLESS_WINDOW_TYPES confirmed from source: 'DOCK,TOOLBAR,MENU,UTILITY,DROPDOWN_MENU,POPUP_MENU,TOOLTIP,NOTIFICATION,COMBO,DND' — the survey list is accurate.

Xpra LOCKED_BATCH_DELAY confirmed as 1000ms default for idle/iconic windows (compress.py: LOCKED_BATCH_DELAY = envint('XPRA_LOCKED_BATCH_DELAY', 1000)). This is the batch delay locked to 1s for minimized windows — it is NOT the auto-refresh timer.

Waypipe per-buffer flicker confirmed by primary source (mstoeckl.com blog): 'each image buffer has its own attached video stream, and the application alternates between buffers' causing quality discontinuity.
Waypipe VAAPI bandwidth confirmed: ~4 MB/s at 80 FPS for 1024×768 on Intel iGPU vs ~130 MB/s uncompressed at 40 FPS.

SCKit queue depth range confirmed (WWDC 2022 session 10155): 3-8 frames (default 3). Rule: process a frame within minimumFrameInterval to avoid delayed frames; release surfaces within minimumFrameInterval × (QueueDepth - 1) to avoid loss. This is a critical constraint not in the survey.

TS_WINDOW_ORDER_HEADER FieldsPresentFlags confirmed (MS-RDPERP §2.2.1.3.1.1): geometry, title, visibility rects, resize margins, show-state, z-order are all separate optional fields gated by individual flag bits — partial updates confirmed.

Batch delay calculation confirmed (batch_delay_calculator.py): focused windows get weight=1 on the 'focus' factor (factor=0 meaning no penalty → lower delay); override-redirect windows similarly boosted; unfocused non-OR windows get min_delay=40ms floor; fullscreen/maximized peer windows force min_delay=100ms.
corrections: CORRECTION — auto-refresh timer default is NOT 1000ms. The survey states 'a GLib timer fires after auto_refresh_delay (default 1000ms of inactivity)'. This conflates two separate values. LOCKED_BATCH_DELAY is 1000ms and governs the batch delay for iconic/idle windows (go_idle() calls lock_batch_delay(LOCKED_BATCH_DELAY)). The auto-refresh timer delay is computed dynamically in update_refresh_attributes() as max(batch_delay×4, min_delay, raw_delay) where raw_delay = sqrt(ww×wh/1e6) × quality_factor × speed_factor × congestion_factor. For a 1920×1080 focused window at quality=65 with no congestion, raw_delay is ~130ms. The timer is scheduled as max(batch_delay×5, min_auto_refresh_delay, base_auto_refresh_delay×sqrt(pct×(1+congestion))/10). In practice this produces delays of 200-600ms on LAN, not 1000ms. The 1000ms figure only applies to batch delay locking for iconified windows, not the auto-refresh firing time.

CORRECTION — RAIL local move/resize: the survey says 'only the final position is sent back to the server via Client Window Move PDU'. Per MS-RDPERP §1.3.2.5 (verified), for mouse-based moves the Client Window Move PDU is NOT sent — only the mouse button-up is sent, and the server infers position from it. The Client Window Move PDU is required only for keyboard-based moves and for resize operations (all resize, regardless of input method). The survey's description is overgeneralized.

CORRECTION — SCKit queue depth constraint omitted from survey. WWDC 2022 session 10155 specifies: to avoid frame loss, surfaces must be released within (minimumFrameInterval × (QueueDepth - 1)). At 30fps and queue depth 3, that is 33ms × 2 = 66ms to release each surface. If VideoToolbox encode takes longer than 66ms (e.g. because the GPU is busy), frames will be dropped by SCKit before they reach the callback. The survey's PATH 2 design needs to set queueDepth=3 (minimum) and ensure the encode-and-release path completes within 66ms. The survey does not mention this constraint.

MINOR CLARIFICATION — survey says Xpra's 'scroll encoding: rolling pixel-hash comparison over 32-pixel blocks'. The actual implementation (video_compress.py) shows scroll detection is part of the video subregion pipeline and is conditioned on FPS being high enough, but the 32-pixel block hash detail is not directly contradicted — it is simply not verifiable from the files read.
pitfalls: SCKit queue depth (3 default) limits how long the encode pipeline can take. At 30fps and depth=3, each surface must be released within 66ms. VideoToolbox HEVC on M-series is typically 2-8ms per frame, so this is comfortable — but do not set queue depth higher thinking it helps latency; it adds latency instead.

VTCompressionSession does not expose a direct 'lossless HEVC' mode via kVTCompressionPropertyKey_Quality=1.0 in all configurations. On macOS, kVTCompressionPropertyKey_Quality is a float 0.0-1.0 where 1.0 is maximum quality for lossy HEVC (4:2:0 subsampling still discards chroma information). True lossless requires either kVTCompressionPropertyKey_Lossless (available on Apple silicon, macOS 12+) or switching to HEVC with 4:4:4 chroma (kCMVideoCodecType_HEVCWithAlpha at full chroma). For the lossless-upgrade pass on text regions, use kVTCompressionPropertyKey_Lossless = kCFBooleanTrue rather than quality=1.0.

UDP for PATH 2 means the lossless-upgrade frame can arrive out of order or be lost entirely. Assign sequence numbers to both the lossy first-frame and the lossless upgrade frame. On the client, if the lossless upgrade is lost, the lossy frame remains the displayed content — which is acceptable (user will see a slightly blurry frame) but must not corrupt the decode state. Do not use HEVC reference frames that span the lossy/lossless boundary; encode the lossless upgrade as an intra (IDR) slice for the dirty region.

CGEvent injection (the project's own risk assessment) for PATH 2 input is not addressed by any mechanism in this survey family. None of the surveyed solutions use CGEvent injection — RAIL routes input over RDP virtual channel, Xpra forwards raw X11 events, Waypipe proxies Wayland input protocol. CGEvent injection on macOS requires Accessibility permissions (AXIsProcessTrustedWithOptions) and is subject to sandboxing restrictions that will block a Mac App Store distribution path.

The lossy-first then lossless-upgrade pattern increases total bytes sent per damage event (one lossy frame + one lossless frame). On a 20ms RTT NetBird link this is fine, but the lossless HEVC frame for a 1920×1080 window can be 500KB-2MB. Budget for this in your UDP send queue; do not let the lossless upgrade starve the next lossy first-frame.

Per-window VTCompressionSession means one hardware encoder instance per captured window. Apple silicon has a fixed number of hardware video encoder instances (typically 2-4 concurrent sessions before falling back to software). If the user has more than 2-4 remote windows open simultaneously, some sessions will use the software encoder path which is 10-30x slower. Detect this via VTSessionCopySupportedPropertyDictionary and warn; consider multiplexing multiple windows into one compression session with dirty-rect metadata as a fallback.
families.conflictsWithCurrent: ["No conflicts with plain TCP for PATH 1 — the mechanism does not touch the terminal path.", "No conflicts with plain UDP for PATH 2 — the mechanism is transport-agnostic; idle-skip and lossy-first both work over UDP. However, the lossless-upgrade frame should be encoded as an intra slice (not using inter-frame references that depend on the lossy frame) to handle UDP packet loss gracefully.", "No conflicts with fps cap 24-30 — minimumFrameInterval at 30fps sets a 33ms capture cadence, and the idle-skip eliminates encode work on static frames, so the cap is effectively 'up to 30fps when content changes'.", "No conflicts with NetBird P2P / no app-layer encryption — the mechanism is at the encode/decode layer, not the transport layer.", "One architectural addition required that is not currently in scope: a separate metadata channel (TCP or a small UDP control channel) must carry window-move/resize/title events separately from the pixel stream, as confirmed by all surveyed solutions. A window move on the host should reposition the client NSWindow immediately via this metadata message, before the next SCKit frame arrives. This is a new component, not a conflict.", "The lossless HEVC path requires kVTCompressionPropertyKey_Lossless which is Apple-silicon-only (M1+, macOS 12+). If Intel Mac host support is required, fall back to quality=0.95 HEVC (perceptually lossless for text at normal DPI) on Intel. This is an implementation detail, not a conflict with the stated architecture."]

families.family: Apple-native low-latency display and control technologies: ScreenCaptureKit, VideoToolbox, Sidecar/LLW, Apple Remote Desktop High Performance, Universal Control, CGEvent injection
families.mechanism: VideoToolbox EnableLowLatencyRateControl four-flag recipe applied to a native VTCompressionSession (not an FFmpeg wrapper), delivering hardware HEVC from SCStream-captured CVPixelBuffers through a parallel capture+encode thread pipeline, with immediate surface release to prevent pool starvation.
families.recommendationPath1: PATH 1 is raw PTY byte-stream over plain TCP. No mechanism in this family applies. Do not add SCStream, VideoToolbox, or CGEvent to PATH 1. The only Apple-ecosystem concern is thread priority: set QOS_CLASS_USER_INTERACTIVE on the PTY read/write loops so the kernel schedules them ahead of background work. All remaining latency is network RTT over NetBird. Ship PATH 1 first precisely because it has no codec, capture, or injection complexity.
families.recommendationPath2: For PATH 2, use a native VTCompressionSession (not FFmpeg hevc_videotoolbox) configured with the exact four-flag recipe verified against the SDK header: (1) kVTVideoEncoderSpecification_EnableLowLatencyRateControl = kCFBooleanTrue in the encoderSpecification dict at session creation, (2) kVTCompressionPropertyKey_RealTime = kCFBooleanTrue, (3) kVTCompressionPropertyKey_ExpectedFrameRate = your target fps (24-30), (4) kVTCompressionPropertyKey_PrioritizeEncodingSpeedOverQuality = kCFBooleanTrue. Use HEVC (kVTProfileLevel_HEVC_Main_AutoLevel) as your codec — it is confirmed to work with EnableLowLatencyRateControl on Apple Silicon, which is your host. Set kVTVideoEncoderSpecification_RequireHardwareAcceleratedVideoEncoder = kCFBooleanTrue to hard-fail rather than silently fall back to software. For SCStream, set queueDepth=5 and configure pixelFormat to kCVPixelFormatType_420YpCbCr8BiPlanarVideoRange for the video path (NV12) — this allows zero-copy hand-off to VideoToolbox without a BGRA→NV12 conversion step; Lumen uses kCVPixelFormatType_32BGRA because it pipes through FFmpeg's avcodec path which handles the conversion, but a native VTCompressionSession accepts NV12 directly. Run a dedicated capture dispatch queue and a separate encode thread with a bounded queue (depth ≤ 3 frames); release each CMSampleBuffer's surface immediately after handing the CVPixelBuffer reference to VTCompressionSessionEncodeFrame. Cap minimumFrameInterval at CMTime(1, 30) matching your 24-30fps target. On the client, use async VTDecompressionSession callbacks, CVMetalTextureCache for zero-copy CVPixelBuffer→Metal texture, and CAMetalLayer.maximumDrawableCount=2 to hold display pipeline latency to one vsync (≤16ms at 60Hz). Add kVTCompressionPropertyKey_EnableLTR=true + the acknowledgement/refresh keys for WiFi client robustness against packet loss — on the WireGuard LAN this is optional but costs nothing.
verifiedFacts: The SDK header (VTCompressionProperties.h, lines 61-65, confirmed across iOS 15.2 through 26.5 SDKs) states the ultra-low-latency recipe as: kVTVideoEncoderSpecification_EnableLowLatencyRateControl + kVTCompressionPropertyKey_RealTime + kVTCompressionPropertyKey_ExpectedFrameRate + kVTCompressionPropertyKey_PrioritizeEncodingSpeedOverQuality. The recipe does NOT include kVTCompressionPropertyKey_MaxFrameDelayCount=0 or kVTCompressionPropertyKey_AllowFrameReordering=false or kVTCompressionPropertyKey_AllowOpenGOP=false — those are valid supplementary settings but are not part of Apple's stated recipe.
kVTCompressionPropertyKey_MaxFrameDelayCount default is kVTUnlimitedFrameDelayCount (-1) per VTCompressionProperties.h line 362-366 (iPhoneOS13.7 SDK). The survey correctly states this.

kVTVideoEncoderSpecification_EnableLowLatencyRateControl was introduced at macOS 11.3 / iOS 14.5 / tvOS 14.5 (confirmed in iPhoneOS15.2 SDK, line 884). The HEVC low-latency path works on Apple Silicon; H.264 low-latency works on both Intel and Apple Silicon. This is a hardware encoder capability, not a header-declared API restriction — the FFmpeg patch (ffmpeg-devel msg185545) explicitly confirms the ARM64 conditional for HEVC.

Lumen (trollzem/Lumen) src/video.cpp lines 1060-1083 confirms: H.264 omits max_ref_frames=1 (the comment reads 'VideoToolbox on Apple Silicon produces all-IDR output when ReferenceBufferCount=1 is set for H.264, causing massive frame drops. HEVC is unaffected and retains max_ref_frames=1'). The survey's description of this bug is accurate.

Lumen PARALLEL_ENCODING is a bit flag (1<<1) defined in src/video.cpp line 292, applied to the videotoolbox encoder at line 1083. The flag decouples the capture thread from the encode session queue — capture thread raises frames to a shared image queue; each encode session pops independently. This is confirmed in the captureThread implementation (lines 1177-1432).

Lumen's SCStream configuration (src/platform/macos/sc_capture.m lines 193-200) sets: queueDepth=5, pixelFormat=kCVPixelFormatType_32BGRA, minimumFrameInterval=CMTimeMake(1, frameRate), showsCursor=YES. The 32BGRA format is used because Lumen pipes through FFmpeg's avcodec path; the nv12_zero_device (src/platform/macos/nv12_zero_device.cpp line 67) shows that when pix_fmt is nv12, it sets kCVPixelFormatType_420YpCbCr8BiPlanarVideoRange on the capture device — so native NV12 capture is available and used when encoding directly via VideoToolbox hw path.

kVTVideoEncoderSpecification_RequireHardwareAcceleratedVideoEncoder is available macOS 10.9+ on macOS, and iOS 17.4+ on iOS (iPhoneOS18.x SDK line 739). On earlier iOS versions it was available from iOS 8 (older SDKs, line 486). This key prevents silent software fallback.

SCStreamConfiguration queueDepth=5 is what both Apple's own sample code (per community reports) and Lumen use. No Apple documentation page returned a numeric default, but the Apple WWDC sample sets queueDepth=5 with the comment 'increase depth to ensure high fps at cost of WindowServer memory'.
corrections: CORRECTION 1 — survey's VT recipe is incomplete and adds unverified flags: The survey lists kVTCompressionPropertyKey_MaxFrameDelayCount=0 and kVTCompressionPropertyKey_AllowFrameReordering=kCFBooleanFalse and kVTCompressionPropertyKey_AllowOpenGOP=kCFBooleanFalse as part of the 'canonical ultra-low-latency VideoToolbox recipe from the SDK header itself (lines 61-65)'. This is false. Lines 61-65 of the header contain exactly four items: EnableLowLatencyRateControl, RealTime, ExpectedFrameRate, PrioritizeEncodingSpeedOverQuality. MaxFrameDelayCount=0, AllowFrameReordering=false, AllowOpenGOP=false are not in the recipe — the header at lines 67-69 mentions AllowTemporalCompression, AllowFrameReordering, MaxKeyFrameInterval, and MaxKeyFrameIntervalDuration as *optional* supplementary controls. The survey conflates supplementary controls with the core recipe and misattributes them to 'the SDK header itself'.

CORRECTION 2 — EnableLowLatencyRateControl ARM64 scope is codec-specific, not API-level: The survey states 'HEVC low-latency rate control requires Apple Silicon (ARM64); H.264 works on both Intel and AS'. This is correct as a behavioral statement but is better framed: EnableLowLatencyRateControl works on macOS 11.3+ on any Mac for H.264; HEVC low-latency mode requires the Apple Silicon Media Engine. The API is available on all macs from macOS 11.3; the HEVC encoder will return kVTPropertyNotSupportedErr when the hardware HEVC encoder lacks the low-latency mode (Intel without T2 chip). This is not a header-declared restriction.

CORRECTION 3 — Lumen does not use SCContentFilter(desktopIndependentWindow:): The survey describes Lumen's capture as SCStream-based but does not note that Lumen captures full virtual displays (CGVirtualDisplay), not individual windows. More importantly, Lumen's actual SCStream pixel format is kCVPixelFormatType_32BGRA, not kCVPixelFormatType_420YpCbCr8BiPlanarVideoRange. For a native VTCompressionSession (not FFmpeg-wrapped) targeting hardware HEVC, you should set the SCStream pixel format to kCVPixelFormatType_420YpCbCr8BiPlanarVideoRange to achieve true zero-copy hand-off; the BGRA path forces a color conversion step inside VideoToolbox or FFmpeg.

CORRECTION 4 — queueDepth default is not documented as '3' by Apple: The survey states 'default 3, recommended 5 for streaming' but Apple's documentation page did not return a numeric default in any accessible form. The number 5 is confirmed from Apple sample code and Lumen. The '3' default figure appears to be community inference, not an Apple-stated value. Treat queueDepth=5 as the correct value for streaming; the 'default 3' claim should be held tentatively.

CORRECTION 5 — survey's Lumen latency figure '1ms encode-to-network' is from Lumen README marketing, not a controlled benchmark: Lumen's README states '1ms encode-to-network latency on M4 over local network'. This figure is plausible for encode throughput but is not a controlled round-trip measurement. Use the ~15ms H264 / ~18ms HEVC encode figures (also from Lumen) as the more defensible numbers; these match the 16.67ms frame budget at 60fps.

CORRECTION 6 — survey attributes the Sunshine HEVC 5K slow-path to 'AV_CODEC_FLAG_LOW_DELAY combined with slice threading': The Sunshine issue #5095 is correctly described as an Apple Silicon HEVC encoder bug triggered by specific FFmpeg flag combinations, but the precise root cause is max_ref_frames=1 for H.264 (causing all-IDR output on Apple Silicon), not a slice-threading interaction. Lumen's fix comment at src/video.cpp:1069 explicitly says 'ReferenceBufferCount=1 set for H.264 causing massive frame drops' — this is the bug. The 73ms figure applies to 5K HEVC with wrong VT flags in general; the specific Apple Silicon H.264 bug is all-IDR output due to max_ref_frames=1, which is a separate (though related) issue.
pitfalls: Surface pool starvation is the primary latency trap on the capture side: if the encode thread holds CMSampleBuffer surfaces past (minimumFrameInterval x queueDepth - 1), WindowServer drops frames. With queueDepth=5 at 30fps, you have 4 x 33ms = 133ms of tolerance — more forgiving than at 60fps. Release surfaces immediately after calling VTCompressionSessionEncodeFrame.

kVTVideoEncoderSpecification_RequireHardwareAcceleratedVideoEncoder must be set in the encoderSpecification dictionary passed at VTCompressionSession creation time, not via VTSessionSetProperty after creation. Setting it post-creation has no effect.

EnableLowLatencyRateControl must also go in the encoderSpecification dictionary (not via VTSessionSetProperty) — it is a VTVideoEncoderSpecification key, not a VTCompressionProperty key. The naming difference (Specification vs Property) is the trap.

On the client iOS side, kVTVideoEncoderSpecification_RequireHardwareAcceleratedVideoEncoder for the decoder equivalent (kVTVideoDecoderSpecification_RequireHardwareAcceleratedVideoDecoder) is available from iOS 8 onward. On the client macOS side, hardware HEVC decode is available from 2017 Intel Macs onward.

CGEvent injection (PATH 2 input) requires Accessibility permission granted before the app launches its injection calls. If permission is absent, CGEventPost silently fails — no error is returned. Build a setup-time permission check with a blocking prompt; do not attempt injection without confirmation.

The 24-30fps cap in your design means minimumFrameInterval = CMTime(1, 30). This is coarser than 60fps; the queueDepth starvation window grows (133ms at 30fps), which is helpful. However, idle-skip logic must explicitly pause SCStream or suppress VTCompressionSessionEncodeFrame calls on unchanged frames — SCStream will still deliver frames at the configured rate on an active display even if content is static.

CGVirtualDisplay (used by Lumen for headless streaming) is a private API. If your PATH 2 host needs to run headless (no physical display), you face the same private API dependency. Consider instead using a physical display or a software display emulator that does not require private APIs; alternatively, mirror Lumen's approach of isolating the private API call in a subprocess.

Your project targets native Swift. VideoToolbox is a C API; it is callable from Swift via the VideoToolbox module but requires careful CFDictionary/CFString bridging for the encoderSpecification dict. Use CFMutableDictionary or a Swift wrapper — do not attempt to pass Swift Dictionary directly to VTCompressionSessionCreate's encoderSpecification parameter.
families.conflictsWithCurrent: No conflicts with current architectural decisions. The four-flag VT recipe is compatible with plain UDP transport (VideoToolbox outputs a CMSampleBuffer containing the HEVC NAL units; you packetize and send over UDP independently). The SCStream + native VTCompressionSession pipeline does not require app-layer encryption (WireGuard handles it). The fps cap of 24-30 is accommodated by setting minimumFrameInterval = CMTime(1, 30) and ExpectedFrameRate = 30. The libghostty terminal path (PATH 1) is entirely unaffected by everything in this family. One thing to note: adopting NV12 (kCVPixelFormatType_420YpCbCr8BiPlanarVideoRange) as the SCStream pixel format rather than BGRA is a deliberate choice that improves PATH 2 but has no effect on PATH 1.

families.family: cross-cutting perception-engineering techniques that make remote feel local
families.mechanism: Client-side cursor overlay (strip from video, transmit position+bitmap as side-channel, composite at display refresh rate) — the Parsec "positional zero latency" technique for PATH 2, combined with Mosh's epoch-gated prediction overlay for PATH 1.
families.recommendationPath1: PATH 1 (libghostty / TCP / PTY): Implement the Mosh PredictionEngine verbatim. On every keystroke, immediately insert a ConditionalOverlayCell or ConditionalCursorMove into a local framebuffer overlay layer. Gate display on srtt_trigger (fires when send_interval > SRTT_TRIGGER_HIGH=30 ms) or glitch_trigger (fires when any prediction stays outstanding >= GLITCH_THRESHOLD=250 ms). Predictions are silently dropped if the server framebuffer disagrees (IncorrectOrExpired path in cull()); the server state wins without a visible flash. Left/right arrow and backspace predictions are covered by the CSI_Dispatch branch in new_user_byte(). Any unpredictable byte (ESC, CSI unknown, CR) calls become_tentative(), incrementing prediction_epoch, so outstanding tentative cells are withheld until a matching confirmed frame arrives. On a 5-20 ms NetBird mesh the SRTT trigger will rarely fire, but the glitch trigger matters: if Claude Code produces a slow response, predictions will appear after 250 ms, providing feedback that input was received. Do not implement SSP transport — PATH 1 uses plain TCP for byte-exact PTY data; the prediction engine runs entirely on the client side as an overlay on libghostty's local framebuffer state, requiring no changes to the TCP stream.
families.recommendationPath2: PATH 2 (ScreenCaptureKit / HEVC / UDP): The single highest-ROI improvement is client-side cursor overlay. In the host capture loop, detect and strip the hardware cursor from each ScreenCaptureKit frame before passing it to VideoToolbox. Transmit cursor position (CGPoint in host-space coordinates) and cursor bitmap as a tiny side-channel UDP message alongside each encoded frame. The client composites the cursor overlay using Metal at display refresh rate, decoupled from the video decode pipeline. This makes the cursor track the local mouse instantly regardless of encode+decode+network latency. Set VideoToolbox encoder properties for low latency: kVTCompressionPropertyKey_MaxKeyFrameInterval = INT_MAX (IDR on demand only), kVTCompressionPropertyKey_AllowTemporalCompression = true, kVTCompressionPropertyKey_MaxKeyFrameIntervalDuration large value, and the equivalent of max_b_frames=0 (HEVC B-frames are disabled by default on VideoToolbox). Drive client-side frame display from CADisplayLink (the macOS VSync source), not from decode completion — hold the last decoded frame when the queue is empty. Apply Reed-Solomon FEC at the UDP packet layer (20% parity overhead) before sending. Embed a capture-to-send timestamp in the frame header (100 µs units) for per-frame latency telemetry.
verifiedFacts: Mosh terminaloverlay.h line 215-225 confirms: SRTT_TRIGGER_LOW=20, SRTT_TRIGGER_HIGH=30, FLAG_TRIGGER_LOW=50, FLAG_TRIGGER_HIGH=80, GLITCH_THRESHOLD=250, GLITCH_REPAIR_COUNT=10, GLITCH_REPAIR_MININTERVAL=150, GLITCH_FLAG_THRESHOLD=5000. All values match the survey.

Mosh terminaloverlay.cc confirm: new_user_byte() handles Print (character insert with rendition matching), CSI 'C'/'D' (right/left arrow cursor prediction), Execute 0x0d (CR), Esc_Dispatch, CSI_Dispatch unknown — all unknown sequences call become_tentative(). backspace (0x7f) is handled in the Print branch, not a separate case.

Mosh terminaloverlay.cc cull() confirms the glitch trigger logic: glitch_trigger is incremented to GLITCH_REPAIR_COUNT (just display) at 250 ms, and to GLITCH_REPAIR_COUNT*2 (display + underline) at 5000 ms. Decrement happens when confirmed quickly within GLITCH_REPAIR_MININTERVAL.

Moonlight pacer.cpp confirms: TIMER_SLACK_MS=3, MAX_QUEUED_FRAMES=3 (pacing queue max), PACER_MAX_OUTSTANDING_FRAMES=5 (3+1+1 for deferred free). handleVsync() waits SDL_max(timeUntilNextVsyncMillis, TIMER_SLACK_MS) - TIMER_SLACK_MS for a frame before bailing — empty queue returns without rendering, leaving previous frame (show-last-frame is implicit). SDL_THREAD_PRIORITY_TIME_CRITICAL for vsync thread confirmed.

Moonlight mouse.cpp confirms: handleMouseMotionEvent drains all pending SDL_MOUSEMOTION events via SDL_PeepEvents in a while loop, accumulating xrel+yrel, before one LiSendMouseMoveEvent call — coalescing verified. Scroll: preciseY * 120 for LiSendHighResScrollEvent, with Q_OS_DARWIN SDL_clamp to [-1.0, 1.0] for both preciseX and preciseY — verified present for both axes.

Sunshine video.cpp confirms: ctx->max_b_frames=0, ctx->gop_size=INT_MAX (for non-LIMITED_GOP_SIZE encoders), ctx->flags |= AV_CODEC_FLAG_CLOSED_GOP | AV_CODEC_FLAG_LOW_DELAY, ctx->flags2 |= AV_CODEC_FLAG2_FAST. These are set unconditionally in make_avcodec_encode_session.

Sunshine stream.cpp videoBroadcastThread line 1408-1409 confirms: ratecontrol_packets_in_1ms = std::giga::num * 80 / 100 / 1000 / blocksize / 8 — targeting 80% of 1 Gbps. send_batch_size = min(64, 64*1024/blocksize) — not a hardcoded 64, it is derived from blocksize with a 64 maximum. Nanosecond-precision timer via platf::create_high_precision_timer() confirmed.

Sunshine stream.cpp confirms frame_processing_latency field in video_short_frame_header_t: stored as little_uint16_at in units of 1/10 ms (100 µs), computed as (duration_us+50)/100. The survey states '100 µs units' which is accurate.

Sunshine stream.cpp confirms frameType field: 1=normal P-frame, 2=IDR, 4=P-frame with intra-refresh blocks, 5=P-frame after reference frame invalidation. Survey lists frameType=4 as 'P-frame with intra-refresh blocks' which is correct.
Sunshine stream.cpp FEC: up to 4 FEC blocks per frame (MAX_FEC_BLOCKS=4), using reed_solomon_encode(). fecPercentage is configurable. The survey's 'up to 4 FEC blocks' claim is verified.

Parsec patent US11750674 (ultra-low latency remote application access) confirmed by USPTO search to describe: removes cursor images from rendered frame stream, generates video stream via video compression, and transmits cursor information comprising cursor location and bitmap separately to the remote browser. This is the correct patent for the cursor side-channel mechanism.

Mosh paper (Winstein & Balakrishnan, USENIX ATC 2012) confirms: Mosh immediately displayed effects of 70% of keystrokes; median keystroke response over EV-DO (3G) was <5 ms for Mosh vs 503 ms for SSH. The survey's claim of '~0 ms perceived' is a characterization consistent with the <5 ms figure on a high-latency link.
corrections: CORRECTION 1 — Mosh FLAG_TRIGGER thresholds omitted from survey: The survey lists only SRTT_TRIGGER_LOW=20 and SRTT_TRIGGER_HIGH=30 as the hysteresis thresholds. The code also has FLAG_TRIGGER_LOW=50 and FLAG_TRIGGER_HIGH=80 which control the 'flagging' (underlining) behavior independently from prediction display. The survey conflates these: predictions are shown when srtt_trigger fires (>30 ms SRTT), but underlining is a separate hysteresis controlled by FLAG_TRIGGER_HIGH=80 ms and FLAG_TRIGGER_LOW=50 ms — not by GLITCH_FLAG_THRESHOLD alone. GLITCH_FLAG_THRESHOLD=5000 ms sets glitch_trigger to GLITCH_REPAIR_COUNT*2, which also enables flagging via the 'glitch_trigger > GLITCH_REPAIR_COUNT' check.

CORRECTION 2 — Survey misidentifies Parsec patent US11706403 as the cursor side-channel patent: The survey cites 'US Patent 11,706,403 (positional zero latency, cursor stripped from video stream, transmitted separately)' as the primary cursor overlay patent. USPTO search confirms US11706403 is actually about gaze-direction-based foveated streaming (adjusting spatiotemporal resolution based on viewer target view direction and transmission latency). The correct patent for cursor stripping and side-channel transmission is US11750674 ('ultra-low latency remote application access'), which explicitly describes removing cursor images from the rendered frame stream and transmitting cursor location + bitmap separately.

CORRECTION 3 — Sunshine send_batch_size is not a fixed 64: The survey says 'up to 64 packets' as if it were a fixed constant. In stream.cpp it is computed as min(64, 64*1024/blocksize), meaning on standard Moonlight packet sizes (~1392 bytes) it evaluates to min(64, 47)=47, not 64. The 64-packet ceiling exists for GSO compatibility but the actual batch size varies by configured packet size.

CORRECTION 4 — Moonlight scroll clamping applies to both X and Y axes: The survey says 'SDL preciseX/Y is clamped to [-1.0, 1.0] to prevent OS momentum scrolling from generating wild deltas over the network' which is correct in intent, but implies it only applies to Y. The code in mouse.cpp applies the SDL_clamp under Q_OS_DARWIN for both preciseY (LiSendHighResScrollEvent) and preciseX (LiSendHighResHScrollEvent) independently.
CORRECTION 5 — Mosh survey says 'AES-256-OCB' but the paper uses AES-128-OCB: The Mosh paper (and implementation) uses AES-128 in OCB mode, not AES-256. The survey's 'AES-256-OCB' claim is incorrect.
pitfalls: The Mosh prediction engine assumes a character-cell terminal model (rows x cols of ConditionalOverlayCell). libghostty exposes a full VT surface but the integration must track confirmed server framebuffer state separately from the predicted overlay. If libghostty does not expose a cell-by-cell diff API, you must maintain a shadow framebuffer for prediction bookkeeping. This is the main implementation risk for PATH 1.

become_tentative() is called on ANY unpredictable byte — including every ESC sequence that is not a simple left/right arrow. Claude Code output is VT-rich (colors, cursor positioning, tmux pane management). The srtt_trigger will frequently fire false predictions that get immediately killed by become_tentative(). On a 5-20 ms mesh the glitch trigger (250 ms) is the relevant threshold, not the SRTT trigger (30 ms). Tune accordingly: consider starting with DisplayPreference=Always for low-RTT links (skip the SRTT gate entirely) since the prediction latency benefit is always present.

The cursor overlay technique (PATH 2) requires ScreenCaptureKit to expose a flag or separate stream for the hardware cursor image so it can be stripped. SCStreamConfiguration has excludesCursorHighlighting but not a blanket cursor-exclusion flag for the composited cursor in all capture modes. Verify that SCStreamConfiguration.showsCursor=false strips the cursor cleanly from captured CMSampleBuffers; if the cursor is already composited into the IOSurface before capture, stripping it requires a separate SCStream cursor stream or coordinate-only transmission with a locally rendered cursor on the client — which is still correct behavior.

PATH 2: On a 24-30 FPS cap with VideoToolbox HEVC constant-quality mode, the encode latency is approximately 5-15 ms per frame on Apple Silicon. The total pipeline (capture→encode→UDP→decode→display) will be ~30-60 ms on a 5-20 ms mesh. At 24 FPS the frame interval is 41.7 ms; a zero-jitter-buffer design means any frame arriving after its VSync slot is skipped and the previous frame is held. This is correct behavior but requires care: ensure the encode+network budget is reliably under one frame interval (41.7 ms at 24 FPS), or raise to 30 FPS (33.3 ms interval).

The Sunshine pacing formula (80% of 1 Gbps) is designed for a GbE LAN. On a WireGuard/NetBird P2P link the effective throughput depends on the overlay MTU and kernel WireGuard path. If the NetBird link caps below 1 Gbps (e.g., on a slower uplink), sending at 80% of 1 Gbps causes burst loss. Measure actual link throughput and set ratecontrol to 80% of actual measured capacity, not hardcoded 1 Gbps.

CGEvent injection (PATH 2 input) is the stated 'single biggest risk' and is not addressed by any mechanism in this family. None of the reviewed solutions (Mosh, Moonlight, Parsec, Sunshine) run on macOS and inject CGEvents as their primary input path. The correct macOS approach is CGEventPost(kCGHIDEventTap, event) which requires Accessibility permissions and works for most but not all applications. Test specifically with apps that use IOKit directly or have their own event loop bypass.

It adds a shadow framebuffer and prediction overlay layer at the client rendering step only.", "No conflicts with PATH 2's plain UDP + NetBird design.
